# Thread: The color blue...........in nature is rare.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T02:24:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172204>

Blue is rare in living nature as it is a color that is associated with organic molecules in alkaline conditions: few living systems have an alkaline chemistry naturally. The morning glory flower, for instance, starts off the day an intense cerulean, fading to an insipid mauve at sundown as the alkalinity in the flower decreases.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T02:25:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172205>

**Ultramarine**

Naturally occurring inorganic blue pigments also tend to be rare, but for different reasons. Before chemistry was well-enough developed as a science to reproduce colors, blue (as well as other colors) had to be dug out of the ground, in the form of lapis lazuli (literally 'blue stone'). This intensely blue, semi-precious stone has been known for millennia: the ancient city of Ur had a thriving trade in it in the 4th Millennium BC. It was believed that lapis was the sapphire alluded to in Biblical writings. It is still mined in Chile, Afghanistan, Burma, Colorado and Siberia.

Lapis was probably most used throughout the 14th and 15th Century in manuscripts and Italian paintings: the pigment was called _ultramarinum_ (ultramarine), meaning 'from beyond the sea'. Being imported it was extremely costly, rivalling or even exceeding [gold](http://h2g2.com/edited_entry/A3327103), and was often referred to as the 'blue gold'. Its use was subject to self-imposed rationing, and the less-expensive and inferior azurite was often substituted, which in itself was subject to shortages and often turned [green](http://h2g2.com/edited_entry/A20218718). It was also a devil to process: the lapis had to be ground finely (not easy, given that it was a rock), mixed with melted wax, resins and oils, wrapped in a cloth, then kneaded under a solution of lye: the fine particles of ultramarine were then collected in a holding vessel while the retained residue held ash and colorless crystals.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T02:32:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172206>

**Why Ultramarine is Blue**
The gold flecks often found in lapis lazuli give a clue to its origins. The tiny gold flecks are iron pyrites, a mineral that forms only in the presence of sulfur. Ultramarine's major constituent elements, sodium, aluminium, silicon and oxygen, do not by themselves lend much color to anything, let alone an intense blue. The colorless [sodalite](http://mineral.galleries.com/minerals/silicate/sodalite/sodalite.htm), a sodium aluminosilicate, results from mixing and heating compounds that contain these raw materials. Add sulfur, and the transformation to ultramarine is dramatic. The sulfur is the color chemist's Philosopher's Stone, transmuting base mineral into something immeasurably more precious. Sulfur does some amazing things to things illuminated: Tensegrity 7 alert.

The sulfur is trapped in the crystal lattice in a special form. Size and shape is a function of thermodynamics and this alters light's interaction. Elemental sulfur, the stuff that goes into fireworks, is a primrose yellow crystalline solid. The sulfur in ultramarine is an altogether more exotic beast. In elemental sulfur, which is stable, has eight sulfur atoms are joined together in a ring. In ultramarine, three sulfur atoms are joined in a chain and carry a surplus, **_fidgety and highly reactive electron_**. It is this itinerant **single electron** that intensely absorbs yellow light, and makes ultramarine very susceptible to the influence of acids, which decolorise it rapidly. This fragile, reactive blue molecule is trapped inside a transparent octahedral cage of aluminium, silicon and oxygen atoms. Nothing can get at it and, being bulky, it cannot get out of its cage: the chemical equivalent of an insect in amber, trapped when the molten sodalite crystallised around it and preserved almost indefinitely from environmental depredation.

Today, ultramarine is still produced and used as a pigment. If selenium or tellurium is substituted for sulphur in its manufacture, intense red and purple pigments are produced. Sounds like Chorophyll and hemoglobin doesn't it.......Mg locked within porphyrin = green and Iron locked within the porphyrin = red

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T02:36:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172207>

**Modern Pigments**
The blue color that we take for granted in this day and age is a comparatively recent invention. Ultramarine, as stated before, had its drawbacks: it was not resistant to acids at all. The main alternative to ultramarine, _Prussian blue, is blue-black_ in colour and decolorised by alkali, and so was useless for frescos as it reacted with the plaster. Chemists had to wait for serendipity to act again on their behalf in the early 20th century.

In 1907, chemists at the South Metropolitan Gas Company in London were trying to react phthalimide with acetic anhydride: a highly insoluble deep blue material of unknown structure was formed. Then in 1927, chemists trying to make dicyanobenzene from copper cyanide and dibromobenzene also produced a similar material.

These chemists had inadvertently discovered the [phthalocyanines](http://www.phthalocyanine.com/). **_These beautifully symmetrical and remarkably stable molecules bear a marked resemblance to the active portions_** of **chlorophyll** and **hemoglobin**, two naturally occurring pigments in biology. In the center of the molecule is a cavity just big enough to accommodate a transition metal, such as copper. _Sounds familiar no?_ Organic molecules are generally thermally unstable, but under the centripetal effect of the metal atom, things do not fall apart and the centre holds perfectly. Copper phthalocyanine is stable to 900°C, and can be precipitated from concentrated sulfuric acid without change. It is resistant to virtually anything that nature or man can throw at it.

Today, phthalocyanines are used in artists' pigments, paint, gas sensors, cancer therapy (**photodynamic therapy with UV light**), detergents and even laser printer cartridges. They are cheap, easy to make, incredibly stable, non-toxic, easily processed and intensely colored. In short, they are a color chemist's Holy Grail. The only disadvantage with these compounds is that their most appealing characteristic as a pigment, their complete insolubility, renders them almost useless as a dye. The chance is high that any blue object you come across will be coated or impregnated with copper phthalocyanine.

Methylene blue however can be used in biology.................

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T02:48:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172209>

So why is blue so rare in nature? The energy of physics is the answer.

There are two ways of reproducing color today: either by absorbing its complementary color (as the eye pigments do) or by generating it directly. If blue was a difficult color to reproduce in pigment, then it is an order of magnitude more difficult to produce it by generating blue light. This is mainly through energetic considerations: **as light becomes bluer,** **_the energy of its constituent photons increases_**, _and the number of materials which can be excited to a high energy state and usefully convert that energy to light diminishes rapidly._

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T03:13:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172213>

This isn't another story about [that dress](http://www.businessinsider.com/white-and-gold-black-and-blue-dress-2015-2), or at least, not really.

It's about the way that humans see the world and how until we have a way to describe something, even something so fundamental as a color, we may not even notice that it's there.

Until relatively recently in human history, "blue" didn't exist, not in the way we think of it.

As the [delightful Radiolab episode](http://www.radiolab.org/story/211119-colors/) "Colors" describes, ancient languages didn't have a word for blue — not Greek, not Chinese, not Japanese, not Hebrew. And without a word for the color, there is evidence that they may not have seen it at all.

**How we realized blue was missing**
In "The Odyssey," Homer famously describes the "wine-dark sea." But why "wine-dark" and not deep blue or green?
In 1858 a scholar named William Gladstone, who later became the prime minister of Great Britain, noticed that this wasn't the only strange color description. Though the poet spends page after page describing the intricate details of clothing, armor, weaponry, facial features, animals, and more, his references to color are strange. Iron and sheep are violet; honey is green.

So Gladstone decided to count the color references in the book. And while black is mentioned almost 200 times and white about 100, other colors are rare. Red is mentioned fewer than 15 times, and yellow and green fewer than 10. Gladstone started looking at other ancient Greek texts and noticed the same thing — there was never anything described as "blue." The word didn't even exist.

It seemed the Greeks lived in a murky and muddy world, devoid of color, mostly black and white and metallic, with occasional flashes of red or yellow.

Gladstone thought this was perhaps something unique to the Greeks, but a philologist named Lazarus Geiger followed up on his work and noticed this was true across cultures.

He studied Icelandic sagas, the Koran, ancient Chinese stories, and an ancient Hebrew version of the Bible. Of Hindu Vedic hymns, he wrote: "These hymns, of more than ten thousand lines, are brimming with descriptions of the heavens. Scarcely any subject is evoked more frequently. The sun and reddening dawn's play of color, day and night, cloud and lightning, the air and ether, all these are unfolded before us, again and again ... but there is one thing no one would ever learn from these ancient songs ... and that is that the sky is blue."

There was no blue, not in the way that we know the color — it wasn't distinguished from green or darker shades.

Geiger looked to see when "blue" started to appear in languages and found an odd pattern all over the world.

Every language first had a word for black and for white, or dark and light. The next word for a color to come into existence — in every language studied around the world — was red, the color of blood and wine.

After red, historically, yellow appears, and later, green (though in a couple of languages, yellow and green switch places). The last of these colors to appear in every language is blue.

The only ancient culture to develop a word for blue was the Egyptians — and as it happens, they were also the only culture that had a way to produce a blue dye.

If you think about it, blue doesn't appear much in nature — there are almost no blue animals, blue eyes are rare, and blue flowers are mostly human creations. There is, of course, the sky, but is that really blue? As we've seen from Geiger's work, even scriptures that contemplate the heavens continuously still do not necessarily see it as "blue."

Read more: [http://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-blue-and-how-do-we-see-color-2015-2#ixzz3gCIK2r4a](http://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-blue-and-how-do-we-see-color-2015-2#ixzz3gCIK2r4a)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T03:14:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172214>

Is the sky really blue? What does that mean?

In fact, one researcher that Radiolab spoke with — Guy Deutscher, author of "[Through the Language Glass: Why the World Looks Different in Other Languages](http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/080508195X/radiolabbooks-20/?tag=bisafetynet-20)," tried a casual experiment with that. In theory, one of children's first questions is, "Why is the sky blue?" So he raised his daughter while being careful to never describe the color of the sky to her, and then one day asked her what color she saw when she looked up.

Alma, Deutscher's daughter, had no idea. The sky was colorless. Eventually she decided it was white, and later on, eventually blue. So blue was not the first thing she saw or gravitated toward, though it is where she settled in the end.

**So before we had a word for it, did people not naturally see blue?**
This part gets a little complicated, because we do not know exactly what was going through Homer's brain when he described the wine-dark sea and the violet sheep — but we do know that ancient Greeks and others in the ancient world had the same biology and therefore same capability to see color that we do.

But do you really see something if you don't have a word for it?

A researcher named Jules Davidoff traveled to Namibia to investigate this, where he conducted an experiment with the Himba tribe, which speaks a language that has no word for blue or distinction between blue and green.

Read more: [http://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-blue-and-how-do-we-see-color-2015-2#ixzz3gCIi8JCp](http://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-blue-and-how-do-we-see-color-2015-2#ixzz3gCIi8JCp)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T03:15:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172215>

When shown a circle with 11 green squares and one blue, they could not pick out which one was different from the others — or those who could see a difference took much longer and made more mistakes than would make sense to us, who can clearly spot the blue square.

But the Himba have more words for types of green than we do in English.

When looking at a circle of green squares with only one slightly different shade, they could immediately spot the different one.

Read more: [http://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-blue-and-how-do-we-see-color-2015-2#ixzz3gCIyLEyj](http://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-blue-and-how-do-we-see-color-2015-2#ixzz3gCIyLEyj)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T03:16:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172216>

Davidoff says that without a word for a color, without a way of identifying it as different, it is much harder for us to notice what is unique about it — even though our eyes are physically seeing the blocks it in the same way.

So before blue became a common concept, maybe humans saw it. But it seems they did not know they were seeing it.

If you see something yet can't see it, does it exist? Did colors come into existence over time? Not technically, but our ability to notice them may have ...

For more fascinating information about colors, including information on how some "super-seeing" women may see colors in the sky that most of us have never dreamed of, check out the [full Radiolab episode](http://www.radiolab.org/story/211119-colors/).

Read more: [http://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-blue-and-how-do-we-see-color-2015-2#ixzz3gCJ7hVTQ](http://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-blue-and-how-do-we-see-color-2015-2#ixzz3gCJ7hVTQ)

### PaulG — 2015-07-18T03:28:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-blue-in-nature-is-rare.16477/post-172218>

[Argyria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Argyria) \- **Argyria** or **argyrosis** (from [Ancient Greek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greek): ἄργυρος_argyros_[silver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silver)) is a [condition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medical_condition) caused by inappropriate exposure to chemical compounds of the element [silver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silver), or to silver dust.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Argyria#cite_note-Andrews-1) The most dramatic symptom of argyria is that the [skin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skin) turns blue or bluish-grey. It may take the form of _generalized argyria_ or _local argyria_. Generalized argyria affects large areas over much of the visible surface of the body. Local argyria shows in limited regions of the body, such as patches of skin, parts of the [mucous membrane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mucous_membrane) or the [conjunctiva](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conjunctiva).

See explanation [http://www.rsc.org/chemistryworld/2012/11/chemistry-blue-man-argyria-colloidal-silver](http://www.rsc.org/chemistryworld/2012/11/chemistry-blue-man-argyria-colloidal-silver)

> Then came the key discovery. When model silver-thiol complexes in a skin-simulating collagen matrix were irradiated with UV light, a photo-oxidation took place. The reaction is a lot like the one used to develop the first photographs. The model skin converted complexed Ag+ to elemental silver, which spontaneously formed Ag-NPs. The pure silver can then be sulfidated and sulfur or selenium exchange reactions produce the very mixture of Ag2S and Ag2Se that is observed in argyrial deposits. All this points to Ag+ as the major bioavailable silver species and indicates the argyrial deposits are not the nanoparticles that were ingested in the first place.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blue\_Fugates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blue_Fugates)

# Thread: Atherosclerosis and the Tensegrity 7 blog.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-and-the-tensegrity-7-blog.16491/>

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T16:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-and-the-tensegrity-7-blog.16491/post-172245>

[https://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-7-black-hole-sun-come/](https://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-7-black-hole-sun-come/)

Then realize that hemoglobin and the arterial tree are designed to have the same spectral frequencies to red light. So this begs the question what happens when the optical density of hemoglobin and the arterial walls change with respect to each other? We call this change a change in the optical density of tissues. This fundamentally changes how both tissues can interact with sunlight just below the skin's surface.

This is very similar to how a leaf controls its photosynthetic capacity in plants.

When the optical density of arteries change in relation to hemoglobin, we can observe a frequency shift in the arterial wall and this leads to a faster ubiquitin marking in the vessel and it ages faster. As it ages its interaction with light changes by inducing a frequency shift in the light it will interact with. The result is a lack of cholesterol sulfate in the vessel wall, in the plasma of blood and in the collagen of the skin. What occurs when light undergoes a frequency shift? It gains weight.

Your head ages faster than your body by evolutionary design because the SCN has to run faster normally than the peripheral tissue clocks. This is why light actually gains weight as it falls to Earth because of a frequency shift in light as it falls to something with gravity. When sulfated cholesterol is missing in your arteries there is also a spectral shift in the arteries that simulates a faster aging luminal wall because the artery also undergoes a frequency shift as the sulfated cholesterol is missed. In essence, your arterial tree begins to age faster than other tissues because of this altered light tissue interaction. The frequency shift in arteries is due to a change in the optical density of the tissues in arteries. All tissues built from DNA have specific optical densities to light. Biology, however, has not thought how these very fundamental interactions might cause atherosclerosis on a chronic slow time scale when over decades sulfation is altered in the skin, blood and arteries.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T16:39:03+0300

Why do all diabetics have some degree of atherosclerosis in the eye that results in neovascularization to try to restore blood flow? Might all diabetics be blue light toxic?

So why is blue so rare in nature? The energy of physics is the answer.

There are two ways of reproducing color today: either by absorbing its complementary color (as the eye pigments do) or by generating it directly. If blue was a difficult color to reproduce in pigment, then it is an order of magnitude more difficult to produce it by generating blue light. This is mainly through energetic considerations: as light becomes bluer, the energy of its constituent photons increases, and the number of materials which can be excited to a high energy state and usefully convert that energy to light diminishes rapidly.

UV-A and IR-A light are needed to make melatonin and melatonin sulfates things in tissues and sulfur can act like a "quibit" in tissues. This sets up the conditions of existence for quick solar recharging in nature (sun and magnetosphere). The Russian data on the correlation of oxygen availability, UV light, and triplet state SO is crystal clear to those who go deeper than just food. Seneff in her own paper says the following, "Unfortunately, there is necessarily collateral damage from superoxide exposure". That alone tells me she does not understand light. She thinks it is a food story. Sorry..........it is not, in my opinion, for atherosclerosis a food story. Triplet state superoxide is required to oxidize the LDL and to catalyze the reaction that produces sulfate from homocysteine thiolactone.

After absorption of light photons, the photosensitizer is first transferred to an excited singlet state by the quantized energy in the photon. Then 3 possible decay channels are possible. This is why quantum physics works on probabilities and not absolutes. The 3 cases are non radiative, radiative singlet state decay to the ground state, and intersystem crossing to an excited triplet state. The last one, may also promote a reaction leading to the singlet ground state by either non radiative decay or by radiative triplet decay. The radiative decays are called fluorescence and phosphorescence, respectively. Life time for fluorescence are on the order of nanosecond time scales where phosphorescence may last up to milliseconds or even seconds. According to Foote (1968), two alternative reaction mechanisms exist for the decay of the excited triplet state which are called Type 1 or 2 reactions. They are characterized by either the generation of free radicals (Type 1) reactions, or the transfer of excitation energy to oxygen molecules directly in a Type 2 reaction.

During Type 1 reactions the triplet state next interacts with a target molecule or protein, other than oxygen. Stop and think about mitochondria now at cytochrome 1 and what should happen seasonally for mitophagy. Type 1 reactions result in the release of free neutral or ionized radicals. We use these to signal. Further reaction with triplet oxygne may also lead to the formation of hydrogen dioxide or superoxide. Where have you heard that before?
In Type 2 reactions, the triplet state of the photosensitizer directly interacts with molecular triplet oxygen (3O2) which is then transferred to an excited singlet state of (1O2). During this reaction the electronic spins are flipped of the electrons in oxygen.

Both reactions are designed to occur simultaneously but when the environment around a mitochondria is altered by pseudohypoxia the Respiratory Quotient can be altered dramatically. The RQ and metabolic rate are synonymns. The reason for this is because all electrons in ECT are designed to move to oxygen for reduction. Their flow is 100% dependent upon the electron density, size of the respiratory chain proteins, and the amount of oxygen present at the end of the respiratory chain. This makes pseudohypoxia within the mitochondria a very powerful environmental variable because it controls the pull of electrons in ECT. Pull in ECT is measured in voltage. So pseudohypoxia means that voltages in mitochodria are falling and that means the magnetic field developed in mitochondria have diminished.

Excited singlet oxygen is very reactive and toxic. It leads to cellular oxidation, mitochondrial swelling and necrosis. Weishaupt in 1968 identified singlet oxygen as the TOXIC agent in photoactivation of tumor cells.

Carotenoids promote the toxic singlet oxygen to triplet oxygen which is **harmless.**...............Does the skin and retina have carotenoids? Yep.

Now.......did you know blue light increases singlet oxygen and decreases triplet? No bueno

Did you know that diabetics, who all get atherosclerosis have no measurable superoxide?

See why now SO and blue light and sulfur and a bad quantum battery are all linked to atherosclerosis?

With pseudohypoxia and blue light exposure you make singlet oxygen over triplet superoxide...........that is the reason why diabetes and most anterior eye diseases are blue light over exposures. This is why we see alterations in Vitamin A in the brain. We are using carotenoids up in tissues chronically exposed in blue light diseases.........and once it is used up there are severe downstream effects..............singlet oxygen predominates in a microwaved blue lit world and you never see triplet superoxide again until you get rid of this bad mitochondria.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T18:41:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-and-the-tensegrity-7-blog.16491/post-172274>

Since type 1 and 2 reaction occur simultaneously, quantum mechanics dictates the path of least resistance. Which probability or mechanism is chosen depends solely on the concentration of oxygen and available triplet oxygen and the appropriate target molecules................
Retina is also loaded with xanthophylls **especially in the macula.**
**The skin is also loaded with carotenoids.**

Seneff mention atherosclerosis is linked to inflammation but she never fully develops it. Why? Cause she is stuck on food........and not light.

The pigments that make apples red and carrots orange—called carotenoids—are an integral part of the body’s immune system and help us fight against aging and other kinds of damage. Clinicians like me place emphasis on seasonal eating of fruits and vegetables, seafood and animal fats because, like other animals, our bodies can’t make these carotenoids and we have to get them from food sources that grow in certain seasons.

We store carotenoids especially in our skin. The outer most layer of skin specifically where LIGHT interacts with them—the stratum corneum—is especially good at absorbing carotenoids. Guess what signal nitric oxide in its dual role in the skin? Endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) is the key with sun exposure. Why? eNOS catalyzes sulfate production in erythrocytes, endothelial cells, platelets and keratinocytes in the skin using carotenoids interaction with the sun's light. In this way, eNOS is a dual-purpose enzyme, that uses UV light as the key metric to either producing sulfate when it is membrane–bound and producing nitric oxide when it is free in the cytoplasm. Why is Aluminum such a bad player in biology? Aluminum reflects UV light more than any other atom on the periodic table........so you can't make the proper amount of eNOS that the sun's light signals for.

The surface chemistry of skin drives the results of the tissues below.............this is where arteries live. This is where atherosclerosis begins.

Why do you need to understand quantum light signaling in your skin, eye, gut in detail? A general feature of most biomolecules is their complex optical band structure between 400-600 nm. Since neither macromolecules or water strongly absorb in the near IR spectrum, what we see, if we observe carefully, is that life, cells and tissues all developed a therapeutic window between roughly 600 nm and 1200 nm of the spectrum of light. This is why near infrared light penetrates most biologic tissues fully. Note, it is light that is the key to cellular construction and why DNA codes for what it does that allows these optical interactions to occur properly. Food is a secondary aspect in this quantized evolutionary design. In this optical spectral range mentioned above, radiation penetrates biologic tissues at a lower loss of energy, therefore enabling the treatment of deeper tissues to the surface tissues. This is how light is used to make Vitamin D3 and how we use sunlight in RBC's, the water fraction of plasma, the cholesterol in that plasma, and the arteries that channel this magnetohydrodynamic fluid all over our body. When the tissue optics are off every signaling cascade distal to it becomes altered to manifest in some disease state. The uniqueness of the absorption spectrum of tissues is unique and it contains the Rosetta stone of how biology really works in my opinion. I view the absorption spectra of tissues with respect to light as the fingerprint of optimal signaling. This is why hemoglobin and artery walls look almost identical..........when they differ you can bet your ass you have atherosclerosis at some level. When you have inhomogeneity in tissues for any reason.........this tells you light and atoms relationship to time is off and time is altered and can be macroscopically measure in telomere lengths and ubiquitin marking levels. Ross Whitehead, Ph.D. candidate at the University of Saint Andrews in Scotland and lead author of new research published in PLoS ONE on the affect of diet on appearance, and his colleagues wanted to know just how _small_ a shift in diet is required to see that healthy carotenoid glow. They followed 35 college students for six weeks, keeping tabs on their diet as well as measuring the color of their skin. What they failed to do is control for the light their skin saw..........but the results they posted were about the color of their skin: Most of you now know color is tied to frequency of light interaction........and this is how foods and sunlight work in unison and why skin color and atherosclerosis maybe linked. If you have yellow and red skin you are more healthy? Why? Yellow is a complementary color of blue light and red light increases RBC's mass and it increases arterial health because both hemoglobin and arterial walls absorb light best in these frequencies..........How is that for homology!

They subjects who, on their own, chose to eat more animal fats or seasonal fruits and vegetables showed increased carotenoid pigmentation on the surface of the skin where light interacts to control the optical signaling of eNOS. So wellness is tied to yellower and redder skin and not a pale skin like your dermatologists will pound into your brain. What color does your skin get with UV light exposure? Sunburns are pink to red are they not? Still think the sun is bad?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T19:07:06+0300

Carotenoids travel through and are stored in our bodies with the help of these most critical fats. Increased blood glyco-hemoglobin A1c levels lead to overestimation of arterial oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry in patients with type 2 diabetes. All diabetics are pseudohypoxic and we seem to not understand why?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T20:05:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-and-the-tensegrity-7-blog.16491/post-172290>

So what do good surgeons and anesthesia personal do when they are taking care of a patient with severe T2D or high blood sugars? They don't rely on a hemoglobin O2 sat and suggest getting an arterial blood gas analysis instead because it will be far more accurate for type 2 diabetic patients with poor glycemic control during the treatment of hypoxemia.

A high HbA1c is a very direct optical sign of severe pseudohypoxia at the skin level and developing at the deep intracellular level around the mitochondrial respiratory proteins.

**Glycation** increases hemoglobin-oxygen affinity and reduces oxygen delivery to tissues effectively. So this menas if you use an optical signal like a pulse oximeter alone to check blood saturation the reading is faulty because while O2 is high in the blood it cannot be released to tissues because of the glycation. This means glycated Hb is less paramagnetic than normal un-glycosylated Hb.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-18T20:14:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-and-the-tensegrity-7-blog.16491/post-172291>

Glycosylated Hb is produced via a non-enzymatic reaction between the free aldehyde group of glucose or other sugars and the unprotonated form of free amino groups of hemoglobin. Today in medicine we know definitively that chronic hyperglycemia accelerates the accumulation of advanced glycation end products (AGE) in the skin collagen, which produces a specific autofluorescence feature. What we don't realize is how this pattern manifests in many diseases. The skin of diabetics is thicker because thick skin is an adaptation to strong light signals in foods. Carbohydrates grow in stronger light seasons and as such it thickens the skin to protect the skin from summer time high UV wavelengths. In diabetics who get thick skin from altered blue lit environments, foods, or technology screens, the thickened skin emits light by absorbing specific wavelengths light and this can interfere with the accuracy of pulse oximetry. Humans should have thicker skin in equatorial or summer time environments and thinner skin at colder polar regions where UV light is sparse by natures design. So if you are a modern human who lives at the pole eating carbs all day and watching Netflix and using your tech gear you can affectively develop T2D and thick sick. If this persists you can eventually develop atherosclerosis via the mechanisms in this thread. T2D is a toxic blue light disease........the carbs are just additive to the fire that blue light causes on our surface tissues.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-05T04:14:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-and-the-tensegrity-7-blog.16491/post-173976>

To understand where I am going you need to understand optics. And not simple optics.

What does Vitamin K2 and porphyrins have in common? = All K vitamins are sensitive to **UV** radiation. Very few people know this or understand why this is key piece of data in for HTN, heart disease and PAD as a surface level disease. This also implies that fake light diminishes Vitamin K. K2 is very sensitive to light and is easily destroyed. This is why K2 has to be with other proteins in a tissue media to work well. This has huge implications for animals who spend most of their time in and unnatural or indoor lit world. ( Macorquodale, Binkley, McKee, Thayer, and Doisy, 1939). You'd be wise to remember that vitamin K2 and CoEnQ10 have a very similar atomic structure. CoEnQ10 is also very sensitive to altered light environments.

Vitamin K2 also protects many cell lines from glutathione depletion making it more important than any glutathione supplement.
[http://www.jneurosci.org/content/23/13/5816.full.pdf](http://www.jneurosci.org/content/23/13/5816.full.pdf)

UV spectra:
UV max 242, 248, 260, 269, 325 nm in ether. UV max (ethanol) 243, 248, 262, 270, 330 nm; IR spectra: (liquid) : 6.05m (CO), 6.21, 6.28m (aromatic nucleus tend to love UV light)
Consider where K2 works.......gut, bone, and vessels. Becker showed us bone uses the photoelectric effect, we know the gut does too, and we know the vessels do as well with the skin. Vessels absorb at the same frequencies as proteins in the blood. 280 mm 420nm 540nm and 580nm and has sharp cutoff occur at 600 nm.

[http://www.jbc.org/content/131/1/345.full.pdf](http://www.jbc.org/content/131/1/345.full.pdf)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-05T04:57:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-and-the-tensegrity-7-blog.16491/post-173983>

An obvious difference between certain compounds is their color. Thus, quinones are yellow; chlorophyll is green; the 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone derivatives (K and K2) of aldehydes and ketones range in color from bright yellow to deep red (similar to carotenoids), depending on double bond conjugation; and aspirin is colorless. In this respect the human eye/skin/gut is functioning as a spectrometer analyzing the light reflected from the surface of a solid or passing through a liquid. Although we see sunlight (or white light) as uniform or homogeneous in color, it is actually composed of a broad range of radiation wavelengths in the ultraviolet (UV), visible and infrared (IR) portions of the spectrum. This is why most of our surface tissues are lined with things that absorbs light in yellows/reds. Plants focus in on the green lights...........both wind up with a DC electric current.

### Jack Kruse — 2022-09-16T02:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-and-the-tensegrity-7-blog.16491/post-315749>

Statins are mitochondrial toxins because of their effect on CoEnzQ10. There are many ways in which they increase coronary artery calcification. One way is depletion of Vitamin K2 from the gut, another is lack of sun to control calcium flows, and another is direct arterial melanopsin damage liberating Vitamin A to cause intimal damage and a loss of arterial NO.

Sufficient production of vital biochemicals such as Geranylgeraniol (GGPP) is required to maintain endotoxin tolerance in macrophages in our arteries once the damage occurs. Macrophages are the hallmarks of CVD/Atherosclerosis, contributing to plaque development, inflammation, and the promotion of thrombosis. Geranylgeraniol is downstream of Mevalonate in the cholesterol synthesis pathway, and GGPP synthesis is inhibited by Statins, as is CoQ10 and K2. Vitamin K2 is the cofactor for matrix Gla-protein activation, which PROTECTS arteries from calcification.

Statin use is independently associated with increased calcification in patients, & using an animal model of hypercholesterolemia, we present a molecular mechanism whereby statins promote the calcification of atherosclerotic plaque. [https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/ATVBAHA.119.313832](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/ATVBAHA.119.313832)

# Thread: No Dreams. None at all
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-dreams-none-at-all.16494/>

### Matty_M — 2015-07-18T19:28:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-dreams-none-at-all.16494/post-172281>

This is something I have danced around a lot, believing it to be a by-product of other issues and something that will slowly resolve itself as my symptoms improve. I have read about it in the Redox Rx, and I have been implementing those practices, however, this is one area in which I lack improvement. I feel very left out and even a little depressed sometimes when my friends or family talk about dreams, while I live eternally in the waking life. I know there is hope, but I'm impatient and very curious....

I believe it is tied to my gut flora (which ain't great). Many months ago, as can be seen in my journal, I got the Genova Diagnostics stool test with a functional doc and we found a parasite, Blastocystis Hominis. She put me on an antibiotic, which I sort of regret, although it elimiated my worst sympmtoms (nasty taste in my mouth) very quickly. She had me on a gut-rebuilding protocol (basically paleo diet + specific supplements for gut health + probiotic pills), but I do not believe it did too much to benefit my gut flora, so the last big push before I leave for Bosnia for 10 months will be the EPCOTx protocol.

Also, the last time I had vivid dreams was when I had a large spoonful of potato starch (resistant starch) in a cup of water. I know Dr. K supports whole foods more, however, is this something I should consider in conjunction with the protocol?

What else might I consider in this circumstance?

\------

Separately, this week when I began to try to reintroduce autoimmune protocol foods (most of which I tolerated well ) I got a little more acne on my skin than normal (so a little acne as opposed to none in the last few months). My "paleo" knowledge tells me it is likely related to the dairy (which was mostly grass-fed, some raw, not all A2 though). What might the real cause be of this? Could it be A1 casein? I feel like acne is always somehow related to my gut flora.

Any input is greatly appreciated.

Thanks,

\- Matt

### Aerose91 — 2016-01-14T02:29:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-dreams-none-at-all.16494/post-186236>

Hey brotha, i know you wrote this months ago but i just read it and want to mention something.

Since getting encephalitis 4 years ago ive been badly dissociated. I cant feel time or reality at all and have basically no recollection of these past 4 years. It feels like i got SUPER high and never came down. I would do absolutely anything to be able to feel reality again and be present, to NOT feel like im deep in a dream. Cherish the feeling that you're constantly in wakefulness and connected, its an absolutely awful thing to lose.

Im not trying to sound like a dick or belittle how you feel, just saying you have something some of us would kill to have back.

I hope you've made some progress since this post though and are well on your way

# Thread: Quotes on nonsense ...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quotes-on-nonsense.16500/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2015-07-19T12:23:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quotes-on-nonsense.16500/post-172343>

This is a little piece from the _Guardian_ that seems to have been related to the Edinburgh Book Festival of about a year ago, but which I only just came across.

It's from the philosopher Roger Scruton at one time better known for controversy over his political views than for his academic work, but in a gentler phase these days. He's certainly a ferociously learned and intelligent man, and a deeply thoughtful one (which is more unusual).

For those who haven't come across him, he's principally worked in aesthetics and is one of the world's leading theorists when it comes to the the aesthetics of music. Light bedtime reading for those whose tastes go that way:

[http://www.amazon.com/aesthetics-music-roger-scruton/dp/019816727X/](http://www.amazon.com/aesthetics-music-roger-scruton/dp/019816727X/)

[http://www.amazon.com/Understanding-Music-Interpretation-Roger-Scruton/dp/1847065066/](http://www.amazon.com/Understanding-Music-Interpretation-Roger-Scruton/dp/1847065066/)

This is good, too:

[http://www.amazon.com/Death-Devoted-Heart-Sacred-Wagners-Tristan/dp/0199928088/](http://www.amazon.com/Death-Devoted-Heart-Sacred-Wagners-Tristan/dp/0199928088/)

Anyway, here's the piece. I think he's saying some interesting things about how he understands art and music and the role of philosophy, but some of this is also quite witty. I don't like all of these, but there's surely something here for almost anyone to enjoy. "The French do it better" is an interesting thought. And I like the gentle mockery of Richard Dawkins:

[http://www.theguardian.com/books/20...ichard-dawkins-original-sin-islamism-and-more](http://www.theguardian.com/books/2014/aug/15/roger-scruton-notes-on-nonsense-richard-dawkins-original-sin-islamism-and-more)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-20T17:31:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quotes-on-nonsense.16500/post-172430>

I enjoyed reading the links. Thanks.

### thomas — 2015-07-20T17:56:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quotes-on-nonsense.16500/post-172432>

Lol it's funny reading "the french do it better" when you're french. If anything, we're just a bunch of dumbasses that just happen to put our cheese in wine then drink it.

### Gagnrad — 2015-07-20T19:49:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quotes-on-nonsense.16500/post-172439>

> [thomas said:](/goto/post?id=172432)
>
> Lol it's funny reading "the french do it better" when you're french. If anything, we're just a bunch of dumbasses that just happen to put our cheese in wine then drink it.

I think there are a lot of layers to it. I think at one level he's just larking around a bit. I also think that while he would recognise and value Sartre's abilities he's also probably poking fun at people like that bit. (And maybe if he were writing a more "political" article he might contrast him unfavourably with someone like Camus.) I also think he simply likes to say "The French do it better" just because he's a francophile and has happy memories of living there as a young man and enjoying French culture in every sense of the word.

But I think he's also quite serious. There's this Anglo-American approach to philosophy that's very rigorous and academic and so much so that it can't say anything significant about life at all. That hasn't been so either in France or Germany.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Leptin Sensitivity and Carbs are Really Confusing Me
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-sensitivity-and-carbs-are-really-confusing-me.16505/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty_M — 2015-07-20T02:49:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-sensitivity-and-carbs-are-really-confusing-me.16505/post-172381>

Hi!

I am constantly confused by whether or not eating carbs for me is optimal, currently. They are definitely in season, I live in southeastern Pennsylvania so it is very hot and sunny here right now, and I spend a lot of time outside.

In "HOW TO EAT POST LEPTIN RX FOR OPTIMAL LIFE" It mentions that when leptin sensitive one can begin to include carbs as up to 30% of one's calories, depending on the season. However, the Leptin Rx blog says that if one is underweight they should eat sweet potatoes with lots of fat. I am not underweight nor overweight, so I am not sure what to do here.

So **what I really can't tell is whether I'm leptin sensitive or not**. It is very confusing.

I'm not overweight or underweight, although I was underweight as a skinny vegetarian before going paleo then epi-paleo then lepin rx and slowly regaining lean mass (now I am very dense- 5' 7" and 160 lbs- it shocks my friends).

Conversely, I do have somewhat of a large appetite (smaller than a few
months ago), and I crave carbohydrates (less than a few months ago) but **is it possible that this is because they are so in season and I'm spending hours out laying in the sunlight?
**
If this does indicate a degree of leptin resistance, is it possible to regain leptin sensitivity while following the protocol and including seasonal carbs- or would one have to remain ketogenic to see results (or, do seasonal carbs just slow the process)? Also, where is the difference between being an optimal fat burner and leptin sensitive? It seems that a qualification for both is to have no carb cravings. Does this mean that someone who is leptin sensitive is also an optimal fat burner?

Months ago, on a salivary cortisol test, I had high morning and high night cortisol. This is stated to be an indicator of leptin resistance.
Since then, I have followed many of Dr. K's protocols and felt this dramatically improve, though I am not currently able to get the test again.

I haven't exercised much out of fear of still being leptin resistant and depleting my stem cells, however, should I do some HIIT's to see whether I feel leptin sensitive (improved recovery, more energy)?

In general, I have noticed an improvement in energy recently, though I can't tell if this is because of all the other protocols or if I am leptin sensitive. Also, I have noticed being more refreshed upon rising.

In the last few weeks, I have been adhering loosely to the leptin rx (less than 50g protein, slightly later breakfast, around 150g of carbs daily) because I adhered very strictly to it for a while (6 weeks) and was pretty sure that I became leptin sensitive.

I do notice that I can make heat pretty well in the ice bath for a good while, so I think I'm pretty cold adapted, which I am under the impression indicated leptin sensitivity.

My skin, nails, teeth,
and face look better than months ago, though my nails still have ridges. I am less explosive in explosive environments, though still not great. I am definitely more sharp mentally, though I feel I could improve here still. My testosterone has increased in about 8 months from around **350** (bottom of reference range) to **1259** (over reference range of <=1000).

**The thing that confuses me most is that I still really want carbs sometimes, and I don't feel as satisfied without them.** When I load up on fat till I'm satiated, often I get a headache or nausea, and in another forum Dr. K suggested I focus on redox and light for this, which I've been doing.

I would really appreciate any observations, advice, thoughts or any other help because this still really confuses me and leaves me unsure about all this stuff.

Off to the ice bath !

Thanks,

\- Matt

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-20T03:54:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-sensitivity-and-carbs-are-really-confusing-me.16505/post-172384>

you answered the question yourself in the first line........Seasons determine food choices for all of human history until 1947 when supermarkets showed up

### Matty_M — 2015-07-20T05:06:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-sensitivity-and-carbs-are-really-confusing-me.16505/post-172395>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=172384)
>
> you answered the question yourself in the first line........Seasons determine food choices for all of human history until 1947 when supermarkets showed up

I see. I thought of this but continued to question it. It seems I will be focusing more on timing going forward, and especially DHA, spring water, sunlight and CT.

It's amazing how much better I feel when away from the Philadelphia suburbs. Reading EMF 7 makes me realize how sped up my life feels on the electrified east coast, compared to last week on Martha's Vineyard. With what I've learned in the last year, this trip to Colorado and Idaho for the next 12 days will be a gift. I'm so grateful for the vacations. I just have to end up living somewhere far better than here.

Thanks a lot doc.

\- Matt

# Thread: THE VITAMIN C THREAD

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-20T06:58:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-vitamin-c-thread.16507/post-172401>

I've been experimenting with high dose vitamin C for the last week or so (5000 - 10000 mg ascorbic acid and/or ascorbate) with very good results. My fatigue is much better and I have more motivation.

Here are some of the things that I have learned about vitamin c:

When someone has adrenal fatigue caused by something, whether it's a virus, mold, candida, heavy metals or any kind of stress, vitamin c is used up at an alarming rate. The adrenal glands use vitamin c to make all your hormones. It's needed to make dopamine. It's vital in the production of collagen.

Here are a couple good videos about vitamin C. They are a must watch:

If you only watch these few seconds of the next video, it will solidify what we already know to be true.. 29:46 - 30:14

### Sue-UK — 2015-07-20T13:08:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-vitamin-c-thread.16507/post-172413>

I'm wondering if its more a UV light thing .... UV light has the potential to clear a lot of things credited to vitamin C (like the toxin producing illnesses), so maybe if a body is able to use light effectively, we would use up less vitamin C, and could take it in smaller amounts, say with the right frequency of light and proper cell signalling directing it to where it is needed?

Its interesting vitamin C competes with the glucose receptors ..... I was also interested in the Inuit using narwhal skin as a source of vitamin C, an ounce having roughly the same as an ounce of oranges ....Without the competition from glucose, perhaps a little goes a long way ...... And a different pathway operates in cold dark and boring? I wonder what high doses of vitamin C signal quantumly? (Summer?)

Does the narwhal's metabolism direct vitamin C to their skin at a particular time of year when they are on top of the water (in the sunlight) heading towards their mating grounds? Are we back to light again ....

### [email redacted] — 2015-07-20T17:00:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-vitamin-c-thread.16507/post-172424>

Capt: I just yesterday happened to be reading Nick Lane's book "Oxygen" and Chapter 9 is all about Vit C it's called "A portrait of a Paradox " Vitamin C and the many faces of an anti-oxidant" I was amazed at how 'complicated' it all is. There is some pretty deep science in that but it pointed out to me or the lesson I would take from it is supplementing is mostly problematic more than likely. But it IS complicated and I could imagine it might well be useful maybe as a needed boost maybe in the relatively short term It sort of for me underlies Dr K's dictum "If we make it we are not designed to take it" though to be strict about this we DON'T make it but our bodies are intricately set up to be able to take it in a 'normal' diet and use it to good effect

To be clear I am not 'advising' you or anything I was more struck by how involved and complicated it is. The science has moved far beyond Linus Pauling's ideas on that one.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-20T17:16:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-vitamin-c-thread.16507/post-172425>

Skin surface biology has an amazing control over the gut............and vitamin C is part of that.

### Sue-UK — 2015-07-21T11:31:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-vitamin-c-thread.16507/post-172496>

Mike I've done high dose vitamin c in the past, and in an emergency I might still do it temporarily, but I've wondered what if not holding on to vitamin c is protective ? or am I using it up at increased rates because I cannot absorb UV? or am I using it up because I can't maintain a charge? What am I missing, doing or not doing, that is causing this? it's interesting that the human body doesn't produce it, and has evolved mechanisms to clear it (urine spilling, bowel tolerance).

I saw an abstract about the development of an iodine, vitamin c/vanadium redox flow battery, it was a bit over my head, but it was about improving symetry and reversibility of the oxidation-reduction system and raising storage ability ......I could imagine a biological equivalent or similar .... So maybe in a biological system it's the DHA, water and iodine that are the key players to either a solar or cold battery, with vitamin c also having a role in charging, say the collagen network? Is using up vitamin c stores too quickly a sign of UV deficiency or inefficiency of the battery? or is it to stop us blowing a fuse .....? Is it protective of DNA if the solar battery construction or how it is working is defective? Is it a sign of DHA / iodine deficiency?

### Amund — 2015-07-20T11:58:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swallowing-your-food-whole.16512/post-172408>

Dr Kruse, I read in your blogpost that we shouldn't chew our food.
[https://www.jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-1-gutcollagen-link/](https://www.jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-1-gutcollagen-link/)

It's proposed that swallowing larger pieces of food increases the amount of subcutaneous fat mass, which is beneficial for brain growth, especially when the brain is still developing.

The blog states that an old man told you this:
‘On the contrary, _**it is not at all necessary to masticate carefully.**_ At your age it is better not to chew at all, but to swallow whole pieces, even bones if possible, to give work to your stomach. I can see that those who have advised you to practise this mastication, and also those who write books about it, have, as is said, “heard a bell without knowing where the sound came from”.’

**1) What makes this man more right than everyone else?** When googling this issue no sites that I found stated any benefits of not chewing your food.
**2) What types of food should and shouldn't be chewed?** Should proteins be swallowed whole, but vegetables be chewed?
**3) At 19 years old is there a point to not chewing, or is the brain fully developed by that time?** I believe it's fully developed at 25, but I'd like to hear what you think.

This is very counterintuitive. Everyone says chew your food. However, I know that the general population often has the wrong idea.

Apologies in advance for not reading through your blog posts more thoroughly... At this time I choose to prioritize my studying. I would still like to know some of the basic facts, because my intuition tells me that you are onto something and I value health and well-being. I am currently doing the leptin reset and a 10 minute cold tap water bath every morning after breakfast.

Thank you for making this information accessible, Jack.

# Thread: Quote from a Japanese healer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quote-from-a-japanese-healer.16515/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2015-07-20T22:44:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quote-from-a-japanese-healer.16515/post-172449>

Quote:

Again the question of hypnosis was brought up, and the professor relayed the question to Jison. Jison replied, and this time, there was instant and direct translation: "Hypnosis is for tricking another person to circumvent conscious volition. Hypnosis is in every case an attack upon an individual's integrity and dignity."

"What is it, then, that you are doing," came the question.

"Something is added not taken away. When there is a clear desire in mind, I only contribute to the cost."

The quote comes from Doug Boyd's book _Mad Bear,_ which I mentioned in an earlier thread:

[http://www.amazon.com/Mad-Bear-Healing-American-Medicine/dp/0671759450/](http://www.amazon.com/Mad-Bear-Healing-American-Medicine/dp/0671759450/)

I just found it very interesting.

I also see it as vaguely within one of the areas that's dealt with here, because it's about people's _energy_.

I'll give the background. Boyd describes Jison as a Buddhist a couple of pages back from this, but he seems to be forgetting, because earlier in the book he had said he was a Shinto priest. However that may be, he seems to have been a very unusual man. His companions say that he had at times meditated for really extended periods - once being picked up and carried in out of a snow drift and set down indoors while still maintaining his focus.

He seems to have been responsible for some quite remarkable healings. Interestingly, the first time we come across him in the book we see him at a conference through Boyd's eyes, and _he_ describes him as nondescript and seemingly very formal and antiquated. But as soon as the Native American medicine man sees him he says he is a man of great power and says he can see snakes around his head.

The dialogue above comes during a demonstration at an institute. It's not a demonstration of healing, because that's something that can't be done for a demonstration. It's stuff like getting a frail girl to balance on a pair of tables, her head on one, her heels on the other, and then load her with weights or get people to sit on her. This seems to be possible after Jison has passed his hands over her. Somehow he is lending energy to her.

At least that is what appears to be happening. Obviously people can believe things like this or not as they choose. For anyone who is intrigued, however, the book is well worth reading. This bit comes right towards the end of the book, but there is interesting material scattered through it, although there are some lengthy and dull passages as well.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-21T01:49:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quote-from-a-japanese-healer.16515/post-172455>

I feel this way about meditation.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-21T01:52:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quote-from-a-japanese-healer.16515/post-172456>

What is added is something so infintesimaly small a simple mind can't fathom is making a difference. Sharing these small things consistently can change the equation.

### Gagnrad — 2015-07-21T21:24:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quote-from-a-japanese-healer.16515/post-172545>

Strange tale. Mad Bear holds a little ceremony before a clinic is built at the invitation of the medical staff there. Part of this, he says, is an understanding that everything in the area - all the animals and even the plants - are involved and their permission is sought for building. He says not one tree must be destroyed. The ceremony involves making a little fire and sprinkling in tobacco as an offering while chanting and saying prayers. Mad Bear invites Jison the Buddhist monk - I erred above: the Shinto priest was a different man - to take part, and he says a prayer in Japanese into the fire.

Mad Bear then tells the clinic staff that the ashes must be gathered up when cold, put in a jar and buried in the foundations.

They begin to walk away. At this point Jison - who previously has never been fazed by anything at all - gets worried. He doesn't like to leave the fire. The Native Americans say it's OK, because Mad Bear has appointed a "fire guard" and point him out. Jison is still not reassured and offers to put the fire out. He then takes off his wooden sandals and his socks and tramples it. Boyd says this is not "ordinary fire-walking". Jison is actually grinding the coals to smithereens with his feet and smothering them. He then picks up the cooled ashes in his hands.

This interested me for a number of reasons, as well as the most obvious one.

It seems to me that both the Native Americans and the Buddhist monk had a similar conception: namely, that once an object (in this case the coals/ashes) had been prayed over they had been given "power" and hence could have a beneficial effect—but could also be misused. Hence the man left as "fire guard", and hence Jison's unwillingness even to leave without the ashes.

I'm not sure anyone in Europe has thought in quite that way for a very long time. In the mediaeval period there used to be locks on the fonts in churches, because it was considered that once the water had been blessed it had a kind of inherent power that could potentially be misused. That I think would be a parallel. This is so unlike the way we think in modern Europe—or North America or Australasia.

The white Americans present were terribly concerned about Jison's feet. Eventually he says to the translator please tell them I am not hurt and I am not crazy.

Later the translator says:

"Now he \[Jison\] is very much thinking about the society in this world. I told you it before. We are getting so strong for technology, but every year the society is becoming weak. So human ability is getting more and more weak and now people only believe machines and they don't develop their body."

I suspect "develop their body" is not quite the right phrase here (or only if taken in some very broad sense). I don't think he's talking about jogging or lifting weights. I think he means something much more along the lines of developing inherent human potential within ourselves. Sure, it could cover not tiring on a hike; it could also cover not having to pile on clothes in winter; but I think it probably also covers abilities we wouldn't even think about, and that many Westerners would not even believe in.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-22T05:44:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quote-from-a-japanese-healer.16515/post-172602>

Human potential is innovation at the most basic form. Ideation means you can still initiate the idea. And when you do it, ask for forgiveness after the fact rather than permission before the fact. Yes, you can still introduce the idea to the world, and yes, there will be many times when the world won’t be ready for your idea. You think I did not know the world was not ready for quantum biology ten years ago? I did but I also knew 2005 wasn’t the Middle Ages either. Great ideas will find an audience eventually, even when the critics and naysayers are powerful. Put your idea out there, and if it’s big and it’s correct, and it mirrors nature's rules, it will stick eventually.

### Gagnrad — 2015-07-22T09:29:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quote-from-a-japanese-healer.16515/post-172615>

I think it's quite nice that Mad Bear perceives the wildlife as having "rights" or "interests" or some kind.

Interestingly, I only discovered a couple of days ago that, unlike in North America, treaties were never offered to the indigenous people in Australia. One person tried it as a private initiative once, and the government immediately quashed it, saying all the land belonged to the Crown.

It seems that Cook had been told to claim any land he found - but only if it were uninhabited. Cook went ahead and claimed Australia anyway, since he considered that the Aborigines didn't own any land, because they seemed to be _moving around_. He explicitly compares them to wild animals. He likely wasn't trying to be rude - and in fact said some complimentary things about them and compared them favourably with the Tahitians - that was simply how he saw them: If you move around you "obviously" have no ownership.

Many of the societies in North America on the eastern seaboard that were originally encountered were settled. They farmed in addition to foraging wild food. So Europeans coming could see their villages and fields and so on, so they would see them as having ownership of the land they were on. Additionally, it probably helped that they were militarily relatively powerful and more nearly a match for the incomers: people also do what they can get away with.

One can see how people thought back then, the Australian professor I heard explain this said, if you look at Locke. Locke said that a man would come to own the land by "mixing his labour with it". Interestingly, this academic points out that Patrick White's _The Tree of Man_, from which Aborigines are absent, begins with such an imagined scene.

I guess he could also have mentioned Rousseau:

_"Le premier qui, ayant enclos un terrain, s'avisa de dire : Ceci est à moi, et trouva des gens assez simples pour le croire, fut le vrai fondateur de la société civil …"_

# Thread: How do Xrays interact with matter in you?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-xrays-interact-with-matter-in-you.16516/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-21T05:25:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-xrays-interact-with-matter-in-you.16516/post-172477>

Xrays speed time up in you by increasing ubiquitin marking to super fast levels.

Since the [quantum energies](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/mod2.html#c3) of [x-ray](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/ems3.html#c4) photons are much too high to be absorbed in electron transitions between states for most atoms, they can interact with an electron only by knocking it completely out of the atom. That is, all x-rays are classified as[ionizing radiation](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/mod4.html#c3). This can occur by giving all of the energy to an electron (photoionization) or by giving part of the energy to the electron and the remainder to a lower energy photon ([Compton scattering](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/quantum/comptint.html#c1)). At sufficiently high energies, the x-ray photon can create an [electron positron pair](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/particles/lepton.html#c6).

### thomas — 2015-07-21T15:28:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-xrays-interact-with-matter-in-you.16516/post-172510>

Love that you're doing the interaction between you and the entire classification of the electromagnetic spectrum.
Xray's And Gamma rays are the highest in energy. They could kill you quickly with a very low dose.. Fortunaly they are filtered by the earth's magnetic field.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-21T15:55:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-xrays-interact-with-matter-in-you.16516/post-172514>

Both increase your ubiquitin rates as their frequency increases............the faster it goes up the quicker you die.

# Thread: How do microwaves interact with your proteins?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-microwaves-interact-with-your-proteins.16517/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-21T05:30:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-microwaves-interact-with-your-proteins.16517/post-172478>

The [quantum energy](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/mod2.html#c3) of [microwave](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/ems2.html#c4) photons is in the range 0.00001 to 0.001 eV which is in the range of energies separating the quantum states of molecular rotation and torsion. The interaction of microwaves with matter other than metallic conductors will be to rotate molecules and produce heat as result of that molecular motion. Conductors will strongly absorb microwaves and any lower frequencies because they will cause electric currents which will heat the material. This turns out to be why biology does not use microwaves as a signal. Microwaves cause molecular rotations and torsions that cause phonons and solitons in cells to alter physiologic functioning. This is because cells are built to be far from equilibrium. High intensity microwaves, as in a microwave oven where they pass back and forth through the food millions of times, will heat the material by producing molecular rotations and torsions. Since the quantum energies are a million times lower than those of [x-rays](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/ems3.html#c4), they cannot produce ionization and the characteristic types of radiation damage associated with [ionizing radiation](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/mod4.html#c3).

### Paleodocteur — 2015-07-21T22:46:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-microwaves-interact-with-your-proteins.16517/post-172563>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-microwaves-interact-with-your-proteins.16517/post-172565>

but to weak to keep them flowing….in a microwavedworld
But we have backup systems don't we? ( good old neurotransmitters and electric circuitry)
revert to be the primate we use to be
we just adapt.

### Paleodocteur — 2015-07-21T22:53:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-microwaves-interact-with-your-proteins.16517/post-172566>

### Lahelada — 2015-07-22T14:04:59+0300

### Scompy — 2015-08-01T19:54:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-microwaves-interact-with-your-proteins.16517/post-173713>

This topic makes me wonder about the meaningful differences between how we digest our food and microwaves, super-heating foods by other means: the impact other sources may have on protein structure. Protein isomers and alterations appear to be very significant to 3D mechanical interactions. I've held ideas for awhile that altereing the protein-key into another slightly altered version, isomer or an unexpected form, would be problematic for the overall key-to-receptorsite-lock **optimal binding-time**. I read a paper awhile back talking about this very thing with L-glutamate vs. another isomer form of glutmate, and that it was suspected that the mirror-version of the molecule had much longer binding times to the NMDA receptor site.

This binding-time idea immediately rose the question in my head, "If glutamate binds longer due to having a higher frequency of altered glutmate molecules sticking longer to receptor sites, wouldn't that be a **huge problem** in some diseases for not turning off neuron interaction?!" There are other proteins that compete for the same type of receptor sites, which would be blocked off too. An example would be taurine that competes for the NMDA receptor site with glutamate.

If an altered glutamate changes timing-function due to environmental influences, this would be a huge issue. And we know it's the ultra-dried out forms and super-heated proteins in processed foods that appear to have the largest reported glutamate-toxicity impact on people... in contrast to the way the body converts glutamtic acid into glutamate.

Related...within the gluten molecule, glutamate is rich (like 40% volume by weight), but the process of making bread by drying out the grain could be why some people claim to have issues with gluten (not just the concentration of glutamate within gluten), but possibly an altered-version of glutamate. So what I'm getting really has to do with the connection of the physics behind the creation of new, altered structures and the ramifications of those interactions.

# Thread: How does infrared light interact with proteins in you?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-infrared-light-interact-with-proteins-in-you.16518/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-21T05:36:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-infrared-light-interact-with-proteins-in-you.16518/post-172479>

The [quantum energy](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/mod2.html#c3) of [infrared](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/ems3.html#c1) photons is in the range 0.001 to 1.7 eV which is in the range of energies separating the quantum states of molecular vibrations. Infrared is absorbed more strongly than microwaves, but less strongly than visible light. The result of infrared absorption is heating of the tissue since it increases molecular vibrational activity at different frequencies in this spectrum. Infrared radiation does penetrate the skin much further than visible light and can thus be used for photomodulation and photographic imaging of subcutaneous blood vessels.

# Thread: How does visible light interact with your body?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-visible-light-interact-with-your-body.16519/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-21T05:41:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-visible-light-interact-with-your-body.16519/post-172480>

The primary mechanism for the absorption of [visible light](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/ems3.html#c2) photons is the elevation of electrons to higher energy levels. There are many available states, so visible light is absorbed strongly by living things. With a strong light source, red light can be transmitted through the hand or a fold of skin, showing that the red end of the spectrum is not absorbed as strongly as the violet end. The violet side contains some serious problems for biologic tissues in excess.

While exposure to visible light causes heating, it does not cause ionization with its risks. You may be heated by the sun through a car windshield, but you will not be sunburned - that is an effect of the higher frequency UV part of sunlight which is blocked by the glass of the windshield.

> They gave out dark glasses that you could watch it with. Dark glasses! Twenty miles away, you couldn’t see a damn thing through dark glasses. So I figured the only thing that could really hurt your eyes (bright light can never hurt your eyes) is ultraviolet light. I got behind a truck windshield, because the ultraviolet can’t go through glass, so that would be safe, and so I could see the damn thing.
>
> Time comes, and this tremendous flash out there is so bright that I duck, and I see this purple splotch on the floor of the truck. I said, “That’s not it. That’s an after-image.” So I look back up, and I see this white light changing into yellow and then into orange. Clouds form and disappear again – from the compression and expansion of the shock wave.
>
> Finally, a big ball of orange, the center that was so bright, becomes a ball of orange that starts to rise and billow a little bit and get a little black around the edges, and then you see it’s a big ball of smoke with flashes on the inside, with the heat of the fire going outwards.
>
> All this took about one minute. It was a series from bright to dark, and I had seen it. I am about the only guy who actually looked at the damn thing – the first Trinity test. Everybody else had dark glasses, and the people at six miles couldn’t see it because they were all told to lie on the floor. I’m probably the only guy who saw it with the human eye.

Feynman’s recollection of his time at Los Alamos and his account of the test of the first atomic bomb in 1945.

# Thread: How does UV light interact with matter?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-uv-light-interact-with-matter.16520/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-21T05:52:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-uv-light-interact-with-matter.16520/post-172483>

The near [ultraviolet](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/ems3.html#c3) is absorbed very strongly in the surface layer of the skin by electron transitions. As you go to higher energies, the ionization energies for many molecules are reached and the more dangerous photoionization processes take place. This distinction in UV is not well understood by medicine. Sunburn is primarily an effect of UV radiation, and ionization is what produces the risk of skin cancer. There is a distinction here that needs to be understood.

The ozone layer in the upper atmosphere is important for human health because it absorbs most of the harmful ultraviolet radiation (UVC) from the sun before it reaches the surface. The higher frequencies in the ultraviolet are [ionizing radiation](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/mod4.html#c3) and can produce harmful physiological effects ranging from sunburn to skin cancer. (Most are also filtered out in different ways) Aluminum is rarely used in biology because it is the one small atom that strongly reflects UV light.

Health concerns for UV exposure are mostly for the range **290-330 nm in wavelength**, the range called UVB. According to Scotto, et al, the most effective biological wavelength for producing skin burns is 297 nm. Their research indicates that the biological effects increase logarithmically within the UVB range, with 330 nm being only 0.1% as effective as 297 nm for biological effects. These frequencies vary daily wth the seasons and photoperiod based upon your location on Earth. So it is clearly important to control exposure to UVB by paying attention to what part of the day your get sun and how much you get during the seasons based upon your location.
