We do Rob........when UBV is strongest it is at summer in 11AM-3 PM. In summer what should you eat? Carbs cause this is when they grow. What do carbs due to RBC's? They raise your HbA1c so that you do not absorb as much UV light. That is the purpose of glycosylated Hb in summer. In winter it forms an antifreeze effect because below 52 degrees insulin does not work as well and this cause a relative glycosylation too. Gilbert Ling found the latter result in 1952.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-30T03:38:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-uv-light-interact-with-matter.16520/post-173475>

You need to understand the history of luminescence: Several investigators reported luminescence phenomena during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, but it was British scientist Sir George G. Stokes who first described fluorescence in 1852 and was responsible for coining the term in honor of the blue-white fluorescent mineral fluorite (fluorspar). Stokes also discovered the wavelength shift to longer values in emission spectra that bears his name. Fluorescence was first encountered in optical microscopy during the early part of the twentieth century by several notable scientists, including August Köhler and Carl Reichert, **_who initially reported that fluorescence was a nuisance in ultraviolet microscopy._** The first fluorescence microscopes were developed between 1911 and 1913 by German physicists Otto Heimstädt and Heinrich Lehmann as a spin-off from the ultraviolet instrument. These microscopes were employed **_to observe autofluorescence in bacteria, animal, and plant tissues. Here again, you have evidence of cells giving off ELF-UV light over 100 years ago._ Amazing how no one today knows this huh?** Shortly thereafter, Stanislav Von Provazek launched a new era when he used fluorescence microscopy to study dye binding in fixed tissues and living cells. However, it wasn't until the early 1940s that Albert Coons developed a technique for labeling antibodies with fluorescent dyes, thus giving birth to the field of immunofluorescence. By the turn of the twenty-first century, the field of fluorescence microscopy was responsible for a revolution in cell biology, coupling the power of live cell imaging to highly specific multiple labeling of individual organelles and macromolecular complexes with synthetic and genetically encoded fluorescent probes.

# Thread: Ven's journal

**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 16

### Venelin Mihaylov — 2015-07-21T21:06:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vens-journal.16536/post-172544>

Hi there,
My name is Venelin Mihaylov, Ven for short  .
So ..., my story, as quick as possible.

I am male, born 1982, 177cm high, 85kg . I live in Sofia, Bulgaria (Sofia is 2mln people city).
My building is a small one, with 6 apartments total. I live on the first floor.

During 2013 my energy levels began to drop and I was not sleeping well. Overall I tend to overexercise, be driven and work a lot. I was using a lot of stimulants to keep me going - vitamins, green tea, cocoa, maca, etc. (dumb) I ate low carb, high protein, high fat (10% carbs, 40% protein, 40% fat), something along those lines. Paleo template, but with commercial meat and veggies. (dumb).
I started getting brain fog and energy crashes. Kept doing what I was doing. More fatigue, more brain fog, more energy crashes followed.
Around spring 2014 I realised that I need to stop the stimulants and give myself time to heal and fix my sleep.
Summer/autumn 2014 I tried to relax, stopped working, exercise to a minimum, social latino dances.
Didn't work. My weight was stable for 10 years at 73kg, but I started putting on weight, and my energy levels were just barely better. Still had brain fog.
I took the issue even more seriously. Increased the fat, dropped some of the protein - 10% carbs, 60% fat, 30% protein, same food template. Didn't work. I was falling asleep around 11-12pm, and was waking up at 4am, and then was awake for 2-3 hours to fall asleep again 7-9-10am. I felt exhausted.

I found out about Dr. Jack Kruse around April 2015. At that point I had gained around 20pounds - I was 82kgs, my clothes didn't fit at all, and I was getting quite depressed about the additional weight. I was feeling quite sick and helpless.

So, I started implementing the leptin rx protocol.
Stopped all evening/night social activities, all night life.
Started eating as soon as possible in the morning, as much as possible.
Started CT 2-3 times a week - around 15 minutes.
food:
salmon
clams
grassfed eggs
lettuce
arugula
some cucumbers
some tomatoes
mct oil
butter
ghee
gluten free bread (sometimes), recent addition

exercise:
1-2/week - swimming, 6x50m max intensity intervals
1-2/week - gym - either complex movements - deadlift/squats, or Tabata protocol for 5 mins. 40min workout
1/week - football - 60x30m field, 8vs8 people, high intensity - 80min
1/week - short hike in the mountain - 1-2hour up / 1-2 hour down
1/week tai chi - 3 hours - Sunday
Total - maybe 5 days with serious physical activity.

Apart from that I do not have high physical activity.

environment:
I avoid using computers/tablet after dark, if I use something, I use glasses with blue filter (60% blue filtered)
I avoid any artificial lights after getting dark, 1 candle at most.
I avoid any work/highly stressful activity after 8pm
I try to go to bed at around 10pm. Sometimes I cannot fall asleep, I get up, have a snack (carb mostly) and then go to bed.
I make my bedroom almost pitch black. I use a night mask when I sleep (big difference for me)

Supplements:
magnesium malate / magnesium orotate - 500mg - 1g / day
licorice root - 3x250mg /day - Solgar-Deglycyrrhized-Licorice-Root-Extract
cod liver oil - 3x15ml / day - [http://goo.gl/5SDbs5](http://goo.gl/5SDbs5)
glutamine +BCAA - 20g BCAA, 10g glutamine, from time to time, when I do not feel full after eating.

Progress:
My brain fog disappeared. I am able to focus much better now, but still have a lot to go to reach my max mental capacity.
When I started the protocol, after about 1 month, I lost around 2pounds weight. After couple of weeks I regained them, and then some. Currently I hover around 85kgs (190pounds)
I have more energy during the day,
Breakfast at 9.30, lunch at 2pm, dinner at 7-7.30pm, good energy between breakfast-lunch, lunch-dinner.
I do not wake up as often in the middle of the night, and when I wake up, I tend to fall alseep faster. Sometimes when I wake up at 4am, I go back to sleep, and sleep light (half conscious) until 9am
Before I was feeling much more jittery and wired and tired. I feel more nicely tired now, tend to feel sleepy earlier in the evening

**Current issues/questions:**
Digestion - often have watery stools, rarely I get a "poopy policeman"  . I sometimes feel bloated (usually around dinner)
I spend a lot of time in bed - often from 10-11pm to 9am next day. But when I wake up, I still feel tired.
After trying to stay at around 60-70% fat, 20% protein, 50-100g carbs per day, I decided to experiment. I increased the carbs to around 200-250-300g/day and my stress levels decreased, I am able to relax better, and get sleepy earlier in the evening. Comments? (Its summer here ...)
**During the day I do not feel really hungry and have good energy levels. But in the evening even when I eat until my stomach is full I somehow feel still not satiated. If I go to bed at that stage, I get jittery and wired and tired. Then I have a high carb snack, and then I feel full, content and ready to go bed. Help me with this please! I am not able to have a 4-5 hours window from last meal to bed.
I am still gaining weight, although much slower, maybe a pound a month ...**

Overall, I have progress, but I am still sick.

I am applying my labs from may 2015. I do not have everything (no cortisol for instance). Unfortunately I did supplement with b complex vitamins and vitamin d before the labs
**Thank you for your time.
Any help/reading suggestion is highly appreciated.**

### Venelin Mihaylov — 2016-06-12T15:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vens-journal.16536/post-196417>

Hi all,
I haven't posted here in a long time, but I did stick with Jack's protocols, for more than a year now.
Thank you very much for your suggestions, to answer the questions:
1\. b612: I use beeswax candles, yes, nothing fancy, the simplest type. I also wear blue-blockers after sundown, along with the candles.
2\. Inger: I ditched the bread, went to sweet potatoes, and then basmatti rice. I can stomach the rice best ..., keep it simple.
4\. Inger: It's a nnEMF hell hole   . There are ~10 wifi networks, 5 telecoms, two of them with 4g networks, etc, etc.    . I am getting a emf blocking bed sheets, see how will that affect me. I am also saving money for a house, but that will take time.
3\. b612: At this point my digestion has improved, I get good excrement around half the time, but I have to be very careful with food, I will implement your suggestions, I feel I still have a long way to get to optimal digestion.
4\. Jack: My light bulbs are incandescent, haven't paid a lot of attention to them, since I use them only accidentally, when I get up to pee, but I guess that it will be good to change them for better ones anyway. I know that even a few minutes are bad for my circadian rhythm.

I havent' updated this thread in a looong time, my bad. I am used to tackle issues on my own, but I am still struggling with this one. Thank you all for your feedback.

My current issue/task to crack:
\- I still wake up in the middle of the night, 5-6 times per week, and have trouble getting back to sleep.
I would say I get hypoglycemia, because I jolt awake and honey + magnesium calms me fast. If don't eat nothing, it gets worse, to the point I can't fall asleep again.
I haven't measured my blood sugar, maybe that would be a good thing to do. I have mostly stable energy during the day, don't get sleepy, do not get energy crashes.

What would be the reason for this? What I could do to get a good night sleep?
Thank you ...

My current practice:
\- big ass breakfast - most often eggs, sometimes beef stew
\- dinner by 7pm - sea food - salmon, clams, etc
\- two meals a day - came naturally around 6 months after I started
\- Stopped rigorous exercise for walking, light yoga, little hiking
\- wear blueblockers after sundown
\- use f.lux at most aggressive settings along with blue-blockers. Most days I finish computer use around 1 hour after sundown.
\- went to 4 hour work day
\- increased time in the sun, time in nature (parks/mountain) to around 2-3 hours a day
\- doing CT for about a year now - cold showers, mountain creeks, cool fat burner, t-shirt in winter. Yesterday I did 30min in around 8-10 degree celsius creek at 1700m elevation. Felt great.
\- bought magnetico - 20 gauss, love it
I have mild astma - 5-10% obstruction. I inhale 0.1mg salbotamol every other night during late spring and summer. I am also allergic to pollens and freshly cut grass, need to take anti allergic drug in late spring, early summer.

When I started, I was spending around 12hours a day in the bed, with around 6-7hours actual sleep - and could barely function. my measure - 1/10 quality.
Currently I spend around 10-11 hours in bed with around 7.5 hours of sleep - sleep quality - 3-5/10. I am able to work around 4-5 hours a day, and take care of myself.

### LisaLearning — 2015-07-21T23:20:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/polymorphic-light-eruptions-photodermatosis.16538/post-172568>

I developed a sun sensitivity about 15-17 years ago after a super sunny holiday. Never had it diagnosed but it looks to be PMLEs.

After many tiring years of slathering my whole life in sunscreen I started taking some melanotan/afamelanotide (synthetic alpha melanocyte stimulating hormone) in the late springtime, got a base tan, which I thought might involve me becoming an itchy, rashy mess, but it oddly didn't. I needed no more sunscreen for normal everyday sun exposures. I mentioned it to my doc (Hertoghe) who gave me his blessing about the whole thing.

I thought it was funny that with the hormone, I don't get any rash, even if I go in a tanning bed and do a short but intensive session, but didn't think too hard about it bc my problem was so much better.

I did the same this spring, although I did it slightly early, and barely got any sun because it's been a cool/overcast summer here, and although I have the normal bit of a base tan from the tanning booth, I got a little sun today, and I am itching again.

It's making me wonder about the hormone itself and whether it alters the photodermatosis, rather than the tan, as I thought previously. Any ideas?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-15T23:26:11+0300

A sun sensitivity is a sign a a bad surface on skin. Tensegrity 7.

Astronauts found something troubling in these shots from space
[http://www.techinsider.io/astronaut-photos-light-polution-l…](http://www.techinsider.io/astronaut-photos-light-polution-led-nasa-esa-2015-8)

... Astronauts aboard the International Space Station are snapping photos of Earth to measure light pollution, and they've found something surprising: Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) — which are touted for their energy-saving properties — are actually making light pollution worse. And the change is so intense that ISS crew members can see it from space.

... But by 2015, after the city transitioned to LEDs, illumination levels in the city center were much brighter than those of the suburbs, with a higher amount of blue light.

Cities around the world have been replacing energy-guzzling streetlights with brighter and whiter energy-saving LEDs. In fact, New York City is retrofitting all of its 250,000 street lights with LEDs in what the city is calling the biggest project of its type in the country. But energy savings does not necessarily translate to happy city dwellers. In a piece in The New York Times, Brooklyn residents complained about the glaring white light creeping into their homes and eyes, causing many restless nights.

LEDs worsen light pollution by giving off more blue and green light than the high-pressure sodium lights they normally replace. And this artificial light pollution washes out the night sky and is linked to many negative consequences. Disrupted night and day cycles can confuse nocturnal animals and alter their hunting interactions, migratory patterns, and internal physiology.

It can also mess with our internal clocks. We produce melatonin at night to help us sleep, which is regulated by light and dark cycles. If we're exposed to light at night, this can suppress melatonin levels, leading to sleep disorders or other problems such as headaches, anxiety, and obesity.

Surfaces chemistry is a big deal not many are aware of. Lisa this includes your famous hormone doc.

Is modern technology destroying our physiology in our brain? [http://ow.ly/QLs5X](http://ow.ly/QLs5X)

yep

Why is alpha MSH messed up in your brain? because your eye skin and gut have unyoked Vitamin A and D3 cycles. Remember A D3 cycles link neuroectoderm. Alpha MSH is made from neuroectoderm tissues.........so your problem will never be solved with a supplement........because it is a mismatched environment causing it. Your famous hormone doc missed that but he is making some nice cash for a half truth. Time for you to get your environment straightened out.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-19T03:26:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/polymorphic-light-eruptions-photodermatosis.16538/post-175081>

Photodermatosis have many photochemical actions within their group as a disease. Sensitivity to the sun has always interested me but I have yet to hack it myself. I believe a serious lack of skin/arterioles Vitamin K2 with alterations in skin Vitamin A and D3 are rarely spoken about. I have a sense this is going to change when we speak about how light interacts on surfaces adjacent to water. I think diseases like rosacea and drug photosensitivity are due to extreme loss of K2 in and around the surface arterioles and capillaries. It brings up the question many ask, does the use of over the counter vitamin K creams or oral K2 really help get rid of bruises and/or tiny broken capillaries on the skin or the face?

There are two studies I am aware of showing that topical Vitamin K cream may help with post-traumatic bruising. In both studies a cream containing Vitamin K was used after pulsed dye laser treatment. As anybody who has underwent treatment with this particular laser, say to rid themselves of unsightly facial blood vessels, knows purpura ( bruising) can be an unwanted side effect. In these studies one by Cohen et al and published in the November 2009 issue of Journal of Drugs in Dermatology, and the other by Lou et al in the December 1999 issue of Dermatologic Surgery, Vitamin K containing creams **_hastened the resolution of this laser-induced bruising._** There are some problems in both studies in that there were other ingredients in the formulations which might have spurred on the body's response ( such as Vitamin C and E) in the former study and Retinol (vitamin A synthetic) in the latter. Further, the number patients in the studies were small and the latter study was funded by the company manufacturing the Vitamin K cream. This for me is a big problem. But I have told people on this very forum when I did my own surgical bio hack I used Vitamin K2, DHEA, and resveratrol orally and topically on my own self induced wounds for this reason. The laser used in these papers likely were too focused and had wavelength drift because the surface temperatures where not controlled well. When I did my bio hack I used cooling of my surface skin any time I used this technique and I always made sure I used natural AM UV and IR light frequencies. All K vitamins have quinones in them so they absorb all frequencies of UV light. Resveratrol absorbs ideally in the UV range as well at 312 nm. Nonetheless, given what I know about wound healing and light, Vitamin K cream does seem to be a worthwhile product for trauma induced bruising. It will allow light to be transferred to the blood plasma to increase energy to the site of damage.
I have not find credible scientific evidence (non manufacturer created) that Vitamin K creams helps with telangiectasias ( the tiny broken capillaries you mention). If you make them with a compounder you can bio hack them yourself. Vitamin C is purported to help wound healing but I am not a buyer that it would work topically. I think IV or food based Vitamin C helps produce strong collagen via its affects on proton tunneling and collagen can help hold the small vessels in place.
If small capillary breaks occur in these vessels, a dermatologist could apply a pulsed dye laser like the KTP laser, electrodessication and IPL might also work quicker than applying a cream. I'd like to do a bio hack in 2016 using topical cream with LED's to see the effect.

Shell fish exoskeletons contain chitin. So add them too your compost too. The earthworms produce chitinase in order to digest the chitin to recycle it and improve the soil that your veggies grow in or that your chickens live upon. The plants then can use the chitinase liberated to the soil to destroy the exoskeletons of insects which are trying to eat the plants. Everything is coupled and quantum and not zen. Pretty amazing mother nature is when you use her abilities and dont fight her. You can also buy or make your own shellfish/crab shell fertilizer. My plan is to add whatever exoskeletons I can get into my compost bin and start growing a wide variety of veggies next year and seeing if the chicken eggs have more DHA content within them as the chickens feed upon this dirt treated with seafood compost. As anybody who has underwent treatment with this particular laser, say to rid themselves of unsightly facial blood vessels, knows purpura ( bruising) can be an unwanted side effect. In these studies one by Cohen et al and published in the November 2009 issue of Journal of Drugs in Dermatology, and the other by Lou et al in the December 1999 issue of Dermatologic Surgery, Vitamin K containing creams **_hastened the resolution of this laser-induced bruising._** There are some problems in both studies in that there were other ingredients in the formulations which might have spurred on the body's response ( such as Vitamin C and E) in the former study and Retinol (vitamin A synthetic) in the latter. Further, the number patients in the studies were small and the latter study was funded by the company manufacturing the Vitamin K cream. This for me is a big problem. But I have told people on this very forum when I did my own surgical bio hack I used Vitamin K2, DHEA, and resveratrol orally and topically on my own self induced wounds for this reason. The laser used in these papers likely were too focused and had wavelength drift because the surface temperatures where not controlled well. When I did my bio hack I used cooling of my surface skin any time I used this technique and I always made sure I used natural AM UV and IR light frequencies. All K vitamins have quinones in them so they absorb all frequencies of UV light. Resveratrol absorbs ideally in the UV range as well at 312 nm. Nonetheless, given what I know about wound healing and light, Vitamin K cream does seem to be a worthwhile product for trauma induced bruising. It will allow light to be transferred to the blood plasma to increase energy to the site of damage.
> I have not find credible scientific evidence (non manufacturer created) that Vitamin K creams helps with telangiectasias ( the tiny broken capillaries you mention). If you make them with a compounder you can bio hack them yourself. Vitamin C is purported to help wound healing but I am not a buyer that it would work topically. I think IV or food based Vitamin C helps produce strong collagen via its affects on proton tunneling and collagen can help hold the small vessels in place.
> If small capillary breaks occur in these vessels, a dermatologist could apply a pulsed dye laser like the KTP laser, electrodessication and IPL might also work quicker than applying a cream. I'd like to do a bio hack in 2016 using topical cream with LED's to see the effect.

HI! I see this post is pretty old. And what am I doing wrong? I just found out via PubMed the other day what the doctor thought was saltwater allergy (yes seriously) I was between 9/10 years old. At my grandparents cottage 20 years ago now. When I first got the "Sun eczema" when it comes. It comes at the same place every time Every year first. I do morning sun. I blueblock. I do some IR. I eat oysters and pretty much what's in your book. The pulpcations doesn't come before after 2-7 days of sun exposure. This summer it's not that bad. But still on my fingers and back and front of hands.and tiny on the arms. Itchy as... I think it's weird because I was so young. Spending time outside. No cell phones. Very little television etc. And that it starts at the same finger every time. There has to an answer ? I've been bugging my mind like crazy. Taking vitamin k2 mk4. Eating lots of fish and veggies. Broth and meat etc. What can this be? I don't itch it anymore. Because I know I'm doing it worse and inflamed then. But it is truly annoying when you feel like you do everything right. And it just doesn't give any answer. They only thing I can think of is that I live in Norway (Bergen) with little sun and long winters
I'll attach the link that made sense to me about this and a photo of my areas. Or marked text. Thanks in advance Link to were I read it here:
[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3063367/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3063367/) kind regards Camilla Rønhovde

# Thread: Weight gain eating Ketogenic

### AnaGF — 2015-07-22T04:21:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-gain-eating-ketogenic.16539/post-172590>

Hello everyone,
I'm hoping someone has some insight into what would be the next step for me.
I've followed a ketogenic diet for a few months now, which I refined as I learned and went along. I came to the diet as a vegetarian for 5 years, which I stopped. I cut all sugars, grains etc and followed a ketogenic eating with plenty of vegetables, grass fed animals, plenty of coconut oil, mct oil and grass fed butter. I also aligned my meals with the circadian rhythm, ate within 30 min.s of waking and even quit coffee for a few months. For about two months I was having hypoglycemia, but that stopped happening since then. I don't have cravings. I was doing yoga 5-6 days a week initially, then I started lifting weights, nothing too heavy, about 4 times per week. As a result of all these changes, I gained about 20 pounds instead of losing, which is what I was hoping. I'm a 30 year old female without any illness that I'm aware of. Rarely get sick, seasonal allergies sometimes. My energy levels are best in the morning and starts to decrease after lunch. Sometimes I have trouble falling asleep, or wake up at night. I measure my glucose before breakfast regularly and it's usually in the 70's but it has fallen lower before and I felt fine.
I've recently become aware of the importance of seafood and I'm including it on my diet along with supplements. I'm curious about what could have caused this and what I can do to change this. Any help would be greatly appreciated. Thanks!

### caroline — 2015-07-22T04:38:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-gain-eating-ketogenic.16539/post-172591>

Hi Ana ....and welcome! This happens to a lot of ladies. Did you take measurements? My weight went up a couple of K's but I went down 2 sizes and then eventually 4 sizes.

Have you tried adding in more fat? and then decreasing the protein? BPC? eats tons of seafood!

Tell us what you are eating and when ....... what water are you drinking?

Have a read of JK's blog ......"after the leptin reset"

are you doing too much?

where do you live? work? nnEMF?????? your postcode matters huge........

You need to get outside as much as possible ...sunlight, sunlight, sunlight! and grounding!

get all electronics out of your bedroom

do you have blue blocker glasses? ....very important

I was doing too much yoga at some point, a very athletic kind, but I'm reducing that.
I live in Dallas, TX. Will definitely be stepping outside much more. Work is relatively low stress (pianist/piano teacher). I do have blue blockers and use flux software…but it seems it's best to shut everything off. I no longer own a TV.

Any time O2 is low and CO2 is high, the cytochromes become more reduced, the respiratory proteins lengthen and ECT slows by factors of ten with every increase in 1 angrstrom; What happens then? AMPK pathways are up-regulated. People perceive the need for carbs but they worsen ROS and RNS signaling. Remember oxygenated blood is paramagnetic and this helps mitochondrial function. Hypoxic blood does not deliver DHA and diatomic oxygen to tissues that need it. Sinclair's paper called this pseudohypoxia...........This is how a Warburg redox shifted metabolism begins acutely.

This means that chronic hypercarbia (Ubi 18 again) destroys magnetic sense in mitochondria (tensegrity 5) from an energy standpoint. High calcium levels diminish magnetic effects within the mitochondria because the chronic elevation of calcium leads to chronic hypoxia. Hypoxia slows ECT transport and up-regulates AMPK pathways and carbohydrate metabolism. When these things occur in unison, DHA is also not delivered to tissues that need it. HEART AND BRAIN ARE ONE AND TWO IN THEIR NEEDS FOR DHA AND O2; (NUGGET ALERT) So that they can change native EMF into electrical signals and back to light in other places within cells. When this situation occurs chronically, circadian signaling is further destroyed within the CNS and within cell membranes system wide and significant disease results.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-22T05:30:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-gain-eating-ketogenic.16539/post-172598>

Summertime is when you turn bad mitochondria to good ones using autophagy and carbs. Ketosis in summer is not always wise and when you say you are gaining weight at this time tells me a lot.

Glycation/dimagnetism affects how much UV light can be stored and carried in RBC mass. UV light is a spring summer time thing.

Pincussen found in 1937 that carbohydrate metabolism is greatly influenced by **_ultra-violet and infrared sunlight in combination_**. (TENSEGRITY 7) The effect is lost when the two forms of electromagnetic energy is delivered separately by artificial means. (QUANTLET ALERT) These forms of sunlight are both coupled in natural sunlight and act to lower blood sugar naturally. This points out why strong sunlight in spring and summer helps offset the blood sugar raising effects of foods that grow during these seasons. Nature couples these effects of sunlight on food to limit RBC and WBC glycation. Today these things are uncoupled in modern humans by our culture and our choices today and reenforced by society's current beliefs. RBC and WBC glycation make these cells less paramagnetic (responsive to magnetic fields in mito) and more dimagnetic (ignores magnetic fields in broken mito) . When this occurs they cannot deliver their payload of DHA and oxygen as they do normally to the cell membranes in any eukaryotic cell (UBI 14 the antenna blog). The affect of glycation alters the iron oxidation state making the hemoglobin more dimagnetic and this means less DHA payload = less UV absorption = lower DC electric current in the tissue like adipose = weight gain.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-22T05:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-gain-eating-ketogenic.16539/post-172599>

DHA is the only chemical that can take any form of light and change it to an electric signal, then back to a light signal within your mitochondria. Don't forget it...........

UV light and DHA are the health see saw................lose one need the another vice versa. Both give you what? DC electric current.......which regenerates all living things.
So does ketosis have a context? YEP. (KETOSIS AND DHA BLOG) (UBI 6 TOO)

Why are summer time high fat diets not a great thing to do chronically across all seasons? High fat diets force the use of mitochondria because they are oxidative powerhouses. But what if your mitochondria functionally suck? If that is the case you must first optimize the things a mitochondria uses to recycle. That is O2 and UV light collection to maintain NAD+ levels at cytochrome 1. What does that mean if you have real bad mito-nuclear coaptation and a poor redox? What does it mean for good coaptation? High carbohydrate diets provide more energy by fermentation, without causing humans actually needing to use their badly functioning mitochondria. Bacteria tend to like this situation. Where are bacteria in you? How to do bacteria and your mitonuclear coapatation work together? Here is another clue: those with mitochondrial diseases who have "adopted, used, and advocated" long term ketogenic diets have been known to collapse into coma because their damaged mitochondria can't provide enough energy needed for a conscious state. They actually cannot function neurologically without help from fermentation. Environmentally broken Paleo advocates use and advocate carbs for that reason. Environmentally broken LCHF folks just get sicker as time evolves because they cannot replace DHA fast enough to offset their environment they allow their mitochondria to face. Some might begin to advocate temporary nicotine use when they are completely stalled and spinning their wheels.........Why might nicotine work? What other cofactors would make nicotine work even better? Nicotine works best with good O2 consumption and strong natural sunlight. Most nicotine users smoke so they never see the optimized effect of nicotine because of co-morbid lung disease from smoking. Most people who are sick rarely go out in the sun because their doctors or their beliefs keep them ignorant of basic physics. So how might nicotine work when O2 and UV light are optimized? Nicotine slightly increases glucose levels to give a small superoxide pulse to make new mitochondria and dig yourself out of the hole called mitochondria senescence. You cannot get the superoxide pulse without the O2 or the UV exposure. Did you know that DHA in your RBC's in skin arterioles 7-10 AM allows you to absorb even more UV light? If you're anemic, hypoxic, or diabetic, your fighting an uphill battle but you can reverse it if you get the details correct. Few people have that discipline. Some of us do. That is why we share what we have learned. All people as they age, they acquire this same kind of mito-nuclear coaptation dysfunction.......the major difference in an older person is the timescale of the decline is slower and results in diseases of aging instead of the neolithic disease today's modern humans get 20-40 years old.

Anyone see a trend here?

The sun is the most powerful drug human can yield and y'all keep running from it...........because of bad science.

Thank you Dr. Kruse for this critical piece of information. I had been doing a ketogenic diet for more than two years. It helped me with a lot of my symptoms (like splitting headaches 3 to 4 times a week, that often led to nausea and vomiting), but I have never been able to 'break the ceiling' to go to the next level. Very occasionally, I would even get these dizzy spells accompanied with diarrhea and shortness of breath whenever my fat intake was especially high in the mornings on an empty stomach. I am happy to report that ever since I added sun gazing and DHA to my regiment two months ago, I no longer have these dizzy spells and diarrhea whenever I unintentionally overdo the fat.
I sleep so much better now, without having to get up in the night several times. I feel so much stronger and energetic. This is turning out to be such an amazing and magical journey for me. My husband and some of my friends have already started to join me. I am so excited. Thank you again.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-22T08:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-high-testosterone-a-good-dhea-level.16544/post-172611>

I just got my testosterone level back today:

24.2 nmol/L (reference range - 8.4 - 28.7 nmol/L)

Since this is not 'free testosterone' does it really tell me anything?

Does it shed any light on my DHEA level?

(I'll have my HS-CRP and Vitamin D3 tomorrow.)

### lilly antic — 2015-07-23T10:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-high-testosterone-a-good-dhea-level.16544/post-172724>

Hi capt.tired,

what you got is then probably the total testosterone. It is bound to SHBG and not in the active form.
It does not say anything about your dhea-s.
you might need to retest these two markers.

### lilly antic — 2015-07-23T10:50:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-high-testosterone-a-good-dhea-level.16544/post-172725>

and estrogen levels are also useful to know.
the aromatase enzyme converts testo in estrogen and vv
If there is plenty of adipose tissue, aromatase is very active and testo gets converted to estrogen at a higher rate.

> Hi capt.tired,
>
> what you got is then probably the total testosterone. It is bound to SHBG and not in the active form.
> It does not say anything about your dhea-s.
> you might need to retest these two markers.

Is all of it bound to SHBG? Just goes to show how much my MD knows (ordering this test)
I know.. it's not her fault. They just haven't been trained properly.
I asked her if she has ever prescribed DHEA. She said they only prescribe testosterone.
She said "Why prescribe a precursor when you can prescribe the real thing (test.)" She was surprised when I said that it is also a precursor for estrogen.
She then offered this test so I took it..any info is good..and insurance covered it

Very little adipose tissue, no 'man boobs' (like Jack gets ...kidding Jack!). I thought that maybe it would give some insight to my DHEA level possibly.
Thanks a bunch for the replies guys!

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-24T04:33:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-high-testosterone-a-good-dhea-level.16544/post-172846>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=172779)
>
> [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/why-mds-struggle-with-qed-fundamentally.16557/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/why-mds-struggle-with-qed-fundamentally.16557/)

Thanks Jack!

# Thread: Being on call or a shift worker:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-22T19:04:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/post-172656>

I know, and I want you to know that a single pulse of the wrong color light at the wrong time of the day initiates circadian phase re-tuning. This destroys your cell and I face it every time I am on call. If you are shift worker you face this too. We know that the composition of cells varies depending on the time of day. Researchers have shown that just one night of poor sleep alters gene expression. [‪#‎LightepigeneticsMatters‬](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/lightepigeneticsmatters?source=feed_text&story_id=945400358857652)[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/…/Just-ONE-night-bad-sleep-alter…](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-3169794/Just-ONE-night-bad-sleep-alter-genes-Pulling-nighter-damages-DNA-biological-clocks-claims-study.html)

[http://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=http:...CgRzopRgSQ2oIqTUPACnXz5aYq7oYrI9pMt85IW5Q&s=1](http://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.dailymail.co.uk%2Fsciencetech%2Farticle-3169794%2FJust-ONE-night-bad-sleep-alter-genes-Pulling-nighter-damages-DNA-biological-clocks-claims-study.html&h=EAQEsq48z&enc=AZOWQx9DBgPj0gilWctqqwonwlIHZpTOHQdR5DhJkwcMSQ1Optua_42KutHQiMcleGptsljZBdsn_YT9Q4YlIed-7gxfpgTsVUxLvXsc3jTBzGW0ePU3W4jv0_XE4GEQtpy4s9YRMni5uxDgjlYnzQW00akAxWDUS9nIgma1gLEXW2fCgRzopRgSQ2oIqTUPACnXz5aYq7oYrI9pMt85IW5Q&s=1)

### jackware — 2015-07-22T19:41:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/post-172665>

I used to periodically pull all nighters to work on writing or just to get a long period of uninterrupted reading time but I no longer do so. I force myself to go to sleep at night and perform all other activities in the day time. I once worked a 3rd shift but quit after 2 weeks. It sucked.

### Shijin13 — 2015-07-22T19:48:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/post-172666>

I can say that my migraines were more intense after working nights, or pulling all nighters for school... if you're going to be doing that, you've gotta put in the extra work to re set your clock.

### thomas — 2015-07-22T20:48:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/post-172670>

That's why I think that sleep always comes first. Then when I am awake, aka conscious me, I can go on with activities.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-22T22:25:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/post-172682>

I just had a 36 yr old shift worker fracture a bone from mowing the lawn. There is so much that is alarming about this but few people can understand how the environment we live in allows two copper ions between the P and N semiconductors of bone to pop out of place and cause apatite to fall off collage to allow this to happen.

### RobH — 2015-07-23T02:44:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/post-172694>

Disturbing... but not surprising to me at this point.

After putting on my (orange) glasses for overnight shift last week the clerk (40 yo 100# overweight, chronic pain, hysterectomy, depression, etc) told me that her new doctor told her to "get those orange glasses or quit the night shift, it is killing you" She had just seen a new doctor in town who told her she had to make major lifestyle changes to get better (eat paleo, get off nights, exercise, etc.) I applauded, got the name of the doctor (who I will refer here), and am planning to talk to him (he's a big Paleo Pusher per his website, but clearly recognizes light as an issue) and will refer him here.

She works FT nights. and has for years. Said she has no interest in making lifestyle changes and "just couldn't" and was going to find somebody new because he wasn't going to prescribe the pain pills she asked for and he told her that in order to be his patient she had to make some changes. I told her again that she was lucky to find him and should do what he said... She laughed and said it wasn't possible. The night wore on...

Now, the astute reader will point out that there is a major problem with this interaction...

RobH

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-23T03:06:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/post-172697>

"She laughed and said it wasn't possible" I hear this shit 4-5 times a week and ocassionally they will complain to the bureaucracy that I did not do what they wanted.

My response is steadfast and disciplined: The only thing standing between you and your goal is the bullshit story you keep telling yourself as to why you can't achieve it. At the end of the day, let there be no excuses, no explanations, no regrets.

### Fractality — 2015-07-23T06:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/being-on-call-or-a-shift-worker.16555/post-172711>

I'm not a shift worker and I'm not suffering from any _manifested_ symptoms of neolithic disease, but I'm beginning to think that I should soon switch to working out in the morning instead of at night. That way, I time my physical exertion with the natural cortisol release and wear my blue blockers as soon as it gets dark outside.

# Thread: Pseudohypoxia vs chronic hypoxia:  They are not the same animal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pseudohypoxia-vs-chronic-hypoxia-they-are-not-the-same-animal.16556/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-22T22:23:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pseudohypoxia-vs-chronic-hypoxia-they-are-not-the-same-animal.16556/post-172681>

PSA: Pseudohypoxia does not equal chronic hypoxia (CH) CH such as we see in sleep apnea or COPD does not affect heart rate that much but has a dramatic effect on HRV. The reverse seems to be true in pseudohypoxia. This is why in a CH state like sleep apnea must be diagnosed and treated aggressively in patients. Likewise, chronic hypoxia induced by rapid elevation gain to high altitude results in a dramatic depression in HRV. The same is true in hypoxic training that some do or in free diving. However, if one climbs to high altitude slowly and acclimatizes, there may be a transient depression in HRV, but it usually returns to normal unless high altitude illness occurs or is also present simultaneously.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-22T22:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pseudohypoxia-vs-chronic-hypoxia-they-are-not-the-same-animal.16556/post-172683>

What does pseudohypoxia mean to our hormone panel? The cost of a high aerobic capacity is low fertility. This is why the pregnenolone steal syndrome exists. Cortisol over sex steroids is due to the use of oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor in human mitochondria.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Why MD's struggle with QED fundamentally?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-mds-struggle-with-qed-fundamentally.16557/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-23T03:58:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-mds-struggle-with-qed-fundamentally.16557/post-172700>

Docs are taught numbers and taught to believe in the numbers diagnostic tests provide much like baseball and football statisticians rely on them to develop an intrinsic value of the player in question. The irony is numbers can never explain science or nature. Math is the alphabet of nature but it cannot explain anything germane to it.

Numbers help justify decisions, remove some risk, and mitigate accountability. **_But they can also keep a great opportunity from being fully considered._**

Einstein understood the limitations of math in a quantum world. I find this level introspection one of his greatest abilities because he saw the limitations of the science and math he helped create.

**Not everything that counts can be counted. Not everything that can be counted, counts.
**
_How do numbers mislead us in medicine and science? They can't explain the influence of the environment in which the numbers describe the probability of what might happen and not what would happen in a specific context of the environment nature is in at that time.
_
The unspoken issue with traditional cold mathematics metrics is that at times, we are so dead set on justifying a clinical decision or an investment in a therapy that we search until we find the metrics that support our decision. That tendency is cleverly spoofed by a series of Geico commercials where a gentleman stands next to a table offering a “car insurance taste test.” On the table are two cups of juice: sample A and sample B. He asks a passerby to take the test, drinking one sample and then the other. Without fail, sample A is “delicious” and “refreshing,” and sample B is “disgusting” and “terrible.” The gentleman tester then reveals that sample A was the Geico sample and sample B was “Other” insurance company. Obviously the taste of juice has nothing to do with car insurance, let alone Geico’s quality of insurance. But the ad makes a good point: sometimes we’ll try anything to prove our labs values or our numbers worth. Geico hopes you’ll also see the satire. Now I hope you see why I rarely get caught up in the numbers of any lab test or battery of labs.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-23T03:59:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-mds-struggle-with-qed-fundamentally.16557/post-172701>

This is why I love MRI's......that picture gives me a real time picture of what labs mean in the present environment.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-23T04:26:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-mds-struggle-with-qed-fundamentally.16557/post-172703>

### Joe Gavin — 2015-07-23T18:42:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-mds-struggle-with-qed-fundamentally.16557/post-172780>

I thought about posting my labs on my journal, from 2013 when I was inflammatory mess to the ones I had done a month ago. Even painstakingly put the values from each year into an excel spreadsheet so "everyone" could comment and tell what me I was doing wrong/right/ whatever.

I came to the conclusion that the only lab value that matters is how my brain is working.

-   Am I sharp?
-   Are big words flowing out easily?
-   Am I thinking with clarity and honesty?
-   Am I emotionally resilient to work and life matters?
-   Do I have the mental strength and courage TODAY to do what I want to do and say what I want to say?
-   Am I living fully and freely?
-   Do I have the strength to Face Everything And Rise (my new definition of FEAR)?

These are the only labs that matter to me.

# Thread: Hans Selye adaptive energy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hans-selye-adaptive-energy.16576/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### René Borg — 2015-07-23T20:12:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hans-selye-adaptive-energy.16576/post-172798>

I was rereading Hans Selye's 'The Stress of Life' and couldn't help but notice that what Selye called 'adaptation energy' seems very similar to what Jack describes as 'redox potential' - as in, the redox potential of an individual would be directly linked to the adaptation energy. This paragraph in particular:

'The length of human lifespan appears to be primarily determined by the amount of available adaptation energy. A better understanding of it promises to show us how to improve recovery from any kind of exhaustion and perhaps even prolong life.Yet all we really know about this mysterious quantity is that constant exposure to anys tressor will use it up.'

Selye wanted to create a unifying theory - seems like its right here in Jack's evolving model.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-23T20:28:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hans-selye-adaptive-energy.16576/post-172808>

Redox is the absolute key to life skating on the second law of thermodynamics. How is not the question

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-07-23T20:53:51+0300

Hans Selye's material is fantastic. He has also written two extremely large (thousands upon thousands of pages with thousands of citations) textbooks on endocrinology and stress. His work should be read by everyone, and combined with Sapolsky's material, forms a rather complete picture of physiological stress and adaptation to it and how it leads to a variety of problems.

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-07-23T21:02:49+0300

A.V. Everitt, W.D. Denckla, and George Washington Crille are others worth reading if one finds the work of Selye and/or Sapolsky interesting. They've shown the potent impact of hypothalamic peptides and hormones, and thyroid hormones, on the "adaptive energy" of an organism, subsequent aromoprhic evolution, longevity, etc. and how the pituitary and adrenal hormones can inhibit these processes. Really interesting stuff.

That being said, all the stuff Dr. Kruse is teaching us about light, magnetism, and water, is related as well and can be used in a practical sense to cause some of the beneficial changes the aforementioned researchers and physicians discovered. For instance, LLLT on the thyroid, genitals, retina, and brain

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-25T07:21:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hans-selye-adaptive-energy.16576/post-172942>

The phylogeny of the hemoglobin protein has shown that the ancestral gene for hemoglobin is ancient (3.8 billion years), and that hemoglobins can serve additional functions besides transport of oxygen between tissues, ranging from intracellular oxygen transport to catalysis of **_redox reactions._** I have told you that the redox potential is the key to life in many blogs. I have also said in the ubiquitination series that certain light frequencies are the key metric in redox potential generation. To many biologists modern beliefs, this seems counterintuitive, but is it really?

UV light has been on Earth from the beginning of the formation of the sun and the planets. So why might the porphyrin ring and hemoglobin so old from an evolutionary perspective? Could it be tied to this primordial relationship between UV light and Earth? We already know that light forms the key basis of all food chains on Earth because it supports photosynthesis doesn't it?

### René Borg — 2016-02-21T18:58:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hans-selye-adaptive-energy.16576/post-189528>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=172808)
>
> Redox is the absolute key to life skating on the second law of thermodynamics. How is not the question

As in 'how' is optimising redox potential - so the better redox, the more adaptive energy we will have over a life-time and the 'more life' we may expect? I ask because of your interesting point on metastability on Barry's thread. We all fall towards chaos as you mention but life maintains us on the edge for all of our lives - so allostasis in the traditional definition is perhaps an inaccurate term because there is no 'fixed boundary' - it moves based on our redox potential at any given moment. Yet, every human ever born has also died - what would you put this down to? Is it simply that while we can optimise our redox in as many moments as possible, even this aspiration can never be perfect - we'll always lose a bit more than we gain over the total time-span of our life - and thus we die. I think of your line 'same older bodies, same scars' - so even if we can optimise our redox to bounce back from more trauma (a la Barry), we can never grow back 'more life' than we lose in the end.

My mind is puzzled by the idea of an organism that could repair itself into every better copies - adaptations without the scars- would such an imaginary being be feasible you think? And if so - what limits it - simply that no environment would be perfect enough to allow it - at least that we know of?

# Thread: Another Seattle-ite
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-seattle-ite.16578/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2015-07-23T22:55:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-seattle-ite.16578/post-172818>

Hi everyone,
(This is a long bio, but it's very worth the reading) ...

I've got some interesting stories from my past that I'll share here as my intor. After listening to every podcast now and reading about 25% of the website, everything is clicking to me and my previous Biology background. I used to work at Stanford U. for many years in the Biosciences Dept and worked as a Chemist at Oregon State U, and I'm failing to find problems with Dr. K's work.

Just as Dr. K truthfully expresses that the environment has changed out from under us with EMF, this is exactly what the food industry has done to all of us as well with specific compounds. I remember the days in the late 1970s when a Twinkie had 5 ingredients. In 1979, I was thin and active as a 9-year old. Everything changed by the age of 12. By then, we had microwave for nearly all meals, I had a heated waterbed (EMFs anyone?), and I was eating Top Ramen (packages of pure excitotoxins). I also flew on airlines 8x per year because my father was a roofer at that time and I got to go on lots of vacations. By the age of 13, I was on my way toward being obese from just three years before it. I staved off some of my bulk playing soccer, which forced me to drink, drink, drink a ton of water during that time.

But by the time of my college years, the fat piled on. By the age of 22 I was around 260 pounds for my 5"10 frame. Something RADICAL happened at the age of 24 in 1994. I took a trip to Finland and spent about 3 months there. During that time, I had to sleep on a waterbed that was unheated. Every morning I woke up, I was pretty cold. So I'd often jump in the sauna (in Finland, sauna's are literally in every home). So after the sauna, I was so terribly hot that I would jump into a 50F pool. Because I had enough bodyfat on me, it felt amazing to do that and there was no cold shock. Then, I'd repeat the process many times over for the next half-hour. So over a good 2 months of doing this, and also eating what I was given--typically soups, breads and in general, lower-carb meals compared to what I was eating in the states, I was literally shocked to find out after returning the US that I weighed 218 pounds (from 260). I knew I had lost a bit of weight (maybe 15 pounds), and at that time I thought it was because I was just eating less food volumes than normal.

I look back on now that feeds directly into Dr. K's protocols with Cold Thermogenesis and his Leptin Rx. But it didn't last being back here in the US. Just two years later at the age of 26, I was 290 pounds. I moved from Stanford to Pasadena, and I had switched from Biology into the Video Game industry. On my first video game project, they moved us to Honolulu for the remainder of the project and I was working on the 34th floor for a good year and when I'd return to my 9th story apartment after working 14-16 hour days, I was often 2am when I went to sleep.

Upon returning to Pasadena, my retinal capillaries began to 'pop' at the age of 28 leaking blood into the gel part of my eye, causing blind spots. I attributed a lot of my retina-bleeding ailments to looking at a computer screen for too long every day (just the overall volume) had didn't have a clue about blue light exposure. My opthamologist told me that he didn't know the cause. As a video game designer, I often worked 12-15 hour days in front of a screen and well, well into the night. We'd often joke about 'burning the midnight oil' and often after working with a bunch of people after midnight, we'd then relax by playing the latest and greatest games til 2am as a common thing. Do the math on me now.

From my biology knowledge and running personal tests on myself, I attributed the blood-vessel popping to the glutamates in my diet, as often after having some kung-pao from a cheap chinese restaurant, I'd get visual issues 30-60mins after the meal that often caused semi-permanent blood-spots for the next day or several weeks. I also figured out that breads, especially bagels, also contributed to the blood-vessel issues along with soynut butter that I was eating at the time too. Protein powders in my 'juice smoothies' would be really bad too. Anything with larger than normal glutamate content including natural sources like aged cheese, sundried tomato, peas, etc. So as you'd expect, I eliminated these from the diet and semi-cured myself by the age of 30ish. The rapid blood-pressure swings were at the root of this, as I was also eating a ton of sugars, being obese at around 270 pounds and running about a 145/100 Blood-pressure at that time.

At the age of 31 just after the 9/11 event happened in NYC in 2001, I had a strange event living in the hub of Pasadena where it felt like i was having a heart attack. I was living alone and called for an ambulance. After they came out, they told me everything was fine, but in the first 30s of testing me, they knew something was going on since my pulse had been crazy high and then came back down quickly. They suspected that something may have 'passed' through my heart like a clot or such. So now I was even more freaked out as you know.

Like other boneheads a year later, I decided once and for all that I'd lose the bodyfat by training for a marathon and then running one. But I had been pretty sick at the age 31 getting pneumonia in both lungs and having a ton of stress and heart arrythmias, so I couldn't make the effort to start marathon training yet.

It was shortly later that I knew I 'had' to run the marathon now, since it was main answer to me at the time. Now, here is the AWESOME thing that I didn't understand until after reading Dr. K. I started getting up with the sun at 6-7am to get out and run when it wasn't so damn hot. I would often run 3-4 times around the Rose Bowl in Pasadena from 7am-10am when it was still cold, but this also zapped me by the end of the day and I'd often go to sleep earlier too. This was in complete contrast to my other pattern of getting up at 9am, getting to work by 10am and leaving work by 10pm-midnight. Also, I'd typically do a really long run on Sundays and it was a common thing for me to go to a role-playing table-top game with friends on Sunday night and gorge on higher-fat foods. What I found was that after a few months of doing the marathon training, I was losing a lot of weight and feeling much better, even though the calories were going through the roof. I attributed it all at that time to just the burning off of calories by running, like a bonehead again.

So by late in 2002, I was able to run the marathon getting down to 220 pounds. This solidified in my brain that running was the answer to everything, like a bonehead. But after the marathon with the carb-feeding, changing my lifestyle to waking up later in the day, running in the evening outside instead of in the morning, etc., the weight piled back on because I was mixing up my circadian timings, and getting back to the same crappy lifestyle again. As you can see, nothing really changed much... I'd still run the same distances, but I wasn't waking up with the sun, wasn't getting that burst of cold in the morning, and now I was getting a ton of blue light again in the evening. The running suddenly wasn't the answer to me... I was pulling out my hair trying to figure out why the running worked during the training, and now it wasn't working anymore. So I attributed it all to food and exercise changes--that it must be a calories in/calories out thing like a bonehead.

### Scompy — 2015-07-23T22:56:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-seattle-ite.16578/post-172819>

The story continues... So I did have some later successes in the future, even though I was still around 250 pounds again. In 2006, Seth Roberts convinced me to try olive oil 2x per day. This actually helped a bunch. Strangely enough, I experiemented around with other oils and found FISH OIL to be the most effective! I did Fish oil for a good 2 years and magically, my weight dropped from 250 down to 190 over about a year. I did wind-sprints in the \*mornings\* again at a nearby park after waking up with the sun because it was the only time of day I could get some exercise in with my video-game work. Irony right?

But at some point, I went back to the Extra light olive oil and thought it would be better. After having my son in 2009, I gained that 'new parent weight' waking up in the middle of the night to help with the wife. By 2010, I moved up to Seattle and was back up to 240 pounds again, even though I was taking the olive oil. This is when I learned about Leangains and Intermittent Fasting and taking in a low-carb diet. From 2011-2014, I was making pretty good progress getting my weight from 240 back down to 185 and then stalled at that point. In late 2013, I did far too much protein, especially later in the day (like 150+ grams per day) thinking it would help, but with the I.F at the same time, I think it stimulated some gluconeogenesis in me due to the excess proteins. Once I figured out it was the excess proteins, this lead me in 2014 to go to strict Ketogenic diet. By the beginning of this year (2015), I had gone from 185 down to 175 stuck between 175-180 with about 20 pounds of fat remaining as an estimation then due to my body comp.

But here is the awesomeness! I've known about Dr. Kruse for about 3 months now, and after absorbing the info, I started these biohacks:

\-- CT in a bathtub 3x per week + cold showers
\-- Eating good-quality seafood daily (incorporating oysters too)
\-- Getting out into the sun immediately upon waking
\-- getting more higher quality water
\-- Blue-blocking glasses & using F.lux on every computer I have

I've been doing this for just 2 months and what has already happened? I'm now at 160 pounds (actually 159), I have more mental clarity, more energy, and I see my abdominal muscles for the first time since the age of 12. I am 45 years old. As you can see with my past and this story and all that I've been through and the experiments I've done on myself, these five things above are really at the heart of the cure for me rather than the myth of food-calories and exercise. What's nice is that because the proof-is-in-the-pudding with me, I absolutely-fricking-know that these things work.

Also, because I am very methodical taking my blood-glucose and blood-ketone levels, even these numbers have improved even though I eat a bit more carb now! I was keeping the carbs to around 10 grams daily, and now I've bumped them up to 20-25 grams and found that my blood-glucose has gotten better! I typically was running in the mornings around a 80-90 blood glucose and a 2.5 mmol ketone, and now I'm running at a 60-70 blood glucose and a 3.5 mmol ketone. A little more carbs but better BG and Ketones because of those 5 biohacks? Seriously, this is amazing and I'm really glad i found Dr. Kruse and we owe him a great deal of appreciation. My entire family is now getting into better health too because I can explain how these mechanisms work to my wife and she's totally on-board too, seeing positive changes as well. Even my 6-year old thinks it's hilarious to throw ice on me in the tub when I'm in it, but he says to me, "I'm way more leptin resistant than you!" rubbing in it and has put his feet and lower-legs in the tub saying it's just fine for him.

The end of the story gets even better. I no longer experience any visual anomoly after eating a higher-glutamate food, and those blood-spots were all reabsorbed away out of my eye naturally so I'm not blind at all. The last time I experienced a visual anomoly (looking at a white wall) was in 2012. Eating low-carb helped me to eat a ton of grassfed butter and meats, which I'm sure helped with the DHA levels. But now with the other 5-biohacks including the seafood, I think these issues are all behind me now. In the past, I must have ultra-low DHA and I was so positively charged in the past that it's really at the basis for the excitotoxicity rather than glutamate itself as the prime source--as we know, calcium burst is where this is a problem similar to the calcium flow after a stroke. By eliminating the glutamate was helpful of course, but was still a bandaid that helped until I realized the true underlying mechanisms.

Thanks all!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-24T04:33:12+0300

Life science research, especially biomedical research, is undergoing a crisis that has arisen because it is now apparent that the published literature is full of biased reporting, under-replication, irreproducible results, under-reported negative results, statistical special pleading, and cruddy primary data collection. # Thread: Light box therapy experiences?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/>
**Subforum:** Heal Your Hormones
**Posts:** 50
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-24T06:40:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-172863>

I'm considering using a light box in the AM before work since the sun is blocked by trees where I live.
I have your typical 'adrenal fatigue' symptoms - very hard to get going in the morning and hard to wind down at night. Anybody had any experiences with them? recommendations?
Thanks in advance.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-24T07:29:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-172869>

Many of the light boxes use blue light which I'm a little hesitant about. I would think that the ideal light box would be one that varies gradually from a reddish spectrum towards- but not actually reaching- a bluish light spectrum..mimicking the rising sun as close as possible.
Jack says that cortisol levels are more effected by the change in light rather than the intensity of it.
I don't think such a box exists but maybe I'm wrong (?)

### Sue-UK — 2015-07-24T13:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-172888>

I don't know of one, but speaking as someone who has a SAD light, (multi, not just blue light), 3 bioptron light therapy units (one large and 2 handheld ones), an infra red and red light and a uv facial tanner, (as well as my own old experimental devices using pre LED stage light filters,gems and goodness knows what else....)  I think one of the problems with light boxes is that they can provide temporary relief but aren't versatile enough long term. I might "feel" better after a session with artificial light therapy but my n=1 is its not on my long term road to optimal, because they don't take into account what else is up for grabs from the real thing. It's tempting to use if its dismal, but I've found that time outside even in the gloom of winter is more beneficial than using an artificial light box. If I get natural sunlight, however dismal, I am getting information about the earth's movement on its axis, and its progress around the sun etc. The sun's spectrum of frequencies are different every day of the year, so whatever combination of artificial light sources I use, they are a mismatch, (but I sometimes use them rather than not get the frequencies at all, usually related to work commitments or because of weather conditions I don't feel safe in). With natural sunlight the changing UV and different frequencies give different seasonal information, which in turn signals changes in physiology. Going barefoot adds to the environmental information gathering. I think scanning the sky helps set the GPS in my head ...etc etc and its free .. I find the experience as a whole is more cortisol friendly than any light box can offer me.

### SeaHorse — 2015-07-24T14:29:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-172892>

^^^what Sue said.....very wise. Using light boxes is like taking a stimulant and thinking you have made good energy. You may feel better, but it is a crap shoot as far as what you have really done to your signalling internally. There was a thread here talking about trying to calibrate light boxes to your latitude or create a different latitude through light...have you had a chance to read it?

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-24T16:18:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-172896>

Is that still better than a light box? The mornings for me is when I really need the energy. Whenever I get a chance I look up towards the sun, especially in the AM, to get that information that Sue was talking about

### Sue-UK — 2015-07-24T17:11:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-172901>

I am now in summer (evidenced by torrential rain ......) and its now I would use a light box if necessary - in the season bright light is naturally available, but sometimes I cant access it.

Because the uk experiences definite seasons, my battery needs to operate differently at different times (Ubi 8, the mammalian battery). I decided that low winter levels here should still switch off melatonin and set the photoperiod, but it wasn't the time for trying to use a device hoping it would provide energy to my battery. It is too poor a substitute, and would confuse signalling. It was more about natural light setting the photoperiod, DHA, water and grounding and CT, with any natural winter sunshine gratefully received .... But a blue light box in winter? Nah - that would be like flipping to the equator and back in an hour ....a blue light carb fest ...... Might be nice for a while but it could jet lag my mitochondria .....

The dielectric constant describes the ability of a material to store energy, whereas, the loss-factor defines energy dissipation of a material. When microwaves are used on a living tissue it becomes dehydrated. Water has the highest dielectric constant in life at 78. When you microwave yourself or your food and you allow a shit load of blue light to hit your retina, gut, and skin while you're doing all this shit at once you are removing potential energy from your life equation.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-27T01:09:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-173154>

Although the accumulation of excess body fat is a complex process with many variables, one variable could be light effect on circadian rhythms. Studies have shown that prolonged dailylight exposure (beyond the normal daily exposure we experience) can increase fat mass through an increase in adipocyte or fat cell size.

Kooijman et al. (2015) conducted a study where three groups of rats were placed under three different daily light conditions to test the effects on fat mass. Rats were exposed to 12, 16, and 24 hours of light each day while caloric intake remained constant. Kooijman discovered that rats exposed to 16 and 24 hours of light had an increase in fat mass despite there being no change in lean mass. Kooijman explained that this was likely due to the lights effects on circadian rhythms.

Circadian rhythms have been known to regulate a wide variety of physiological as well as metabolic processes in the body. These processes range from digestion and hormone release, to the onset of sleep and eating patterns.

While natural light during normal hours of the day is benefical, we should aim to decrease our exposure to artifical light at night, which most of us now get via the use of computers, TVs, cell phones, and tablets.

In a discovery that could question Einstein's longstanding fundamental laws of the universe, an international team of scientists announced Thursday that they had successfully recorded subatomic particles traveling at speeds higher than light.
Antonio Ereditato, spokesman for the researchers, told Reuters that neutrinos pumped from CERN near Geneva to Gran Sasso in Italy had arrived 60 nanoseconds quicker than light, according to measurements taken over three years.

### Sue-UK — 2015-07-28T20:47:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-173332>

I've been rereading Lyall Watson's book Supernature. (1973), he mentions that two American Air Force scientists had recently shown that it was possible to influence the cycle with an artificial moon. They selected twenty women with a history of chronic menstrual irregularity, and persuaded them to keep their bedroom lights on all night on the three days closest to ovulation. All the women menstruated exactly 14 days later. It also said that the light of the moon is 300,000 times less bright than the sun but life is able to respond to this minute cosmic stimulus ..... Maybe our use of technology is also creating weird moon phases at night..... Moonlight therapy .....?

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-28T21:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-173340>

So this morning I tried a small light box that my wife borrowed for me from a co-worker who wants to sell it. I turned it on and I was completely blasted with blue light! And it was BLUE!
It triggered a migraine. I could feel the warning signs almost immediately (forehead pressure).
I went to work but had to leave after three hours. It's a good thing that I live so close to work.
I don't know if the blue light triggered the migraine or my knowledge of how bad blue light is with the resulting thought:"what the hell are you doing?" that triggered it. Probably a little of both.
I won't be buying this thing. I've never liked blue light anyway, even before I knew about the health effects. People say that it's suppose to help with depression but I don't think it would for me. If it gives me a headache that would lead me to believe that it reduces serotonin. At least in me.
I've always preferred morning and evening outdoor light. I think photographers call it the 'sweet light' because of how it really brings out the colours in things. Just look at the trees in the evening. Beautiful...

The book also says that the Hughes Aircraft lab in California had developed a "tilt meter" so sensitive that it had been able to record lunar tides in a cup of tea. Thinking whole body, through the hydrogen bonding network?

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-29T01:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-173355>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=173344)
>
> The book also says that the Hughes Aircraft lab in California had developed a "tilt meter" so sensitive that it had been able to record lunar tides in a cup of tea. Thinking whole body, through the hydrogen bonding network?

Holy ..and that was back in '73!

### Jackie Nelson — 2019-03-19T04:44:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-265850>

Did you use the blue light box in a very DARK room? Those devices should be used in well lit rooms otherwise headache can be a common side
effect. As mentioned before, I believe natural morning light is far superior to a light box BUT when it's still dark on the way to work and all you can
get is horrid bright LED's as soon as you enter the building, perhaps a bit of light therapy beforehand can help. I'm currently experimenting with the Luminette SAD glasses. Maybe wearing red lens glasses while using the luminette could help? Again, I dislike artificial, but SAD and serious winter depression are very real and have been significantly helped with light therapy devices. Not sure if Jack is implying that your life span is threatened, but frankly, I prefer to have some good quality of life and die a bit earlier than to suffer through SAD and poor sleep all winter.

### Ronald Waters — 2019-03-19T04:59:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-265852>

> [Peertje80 said:](/goto/post?id=173188)
>
> Is there a difference between a fully lit room or tiny dimmed light? Or is it just light and dark and nothing in between when it comes down to fake light?

The artificial light is on a continuum. None is best and flooded in it is worst. Also the higher the frequency the light the worse it is. If you need to be exposed to artificial light cover your skin and wear blue light blocking glasses.

### Jackie Nelson — 2019-03-19T21:49:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-265930>

I have added red light to my luminette glasses by covering some of the diodes with red cellophane. May sound crazy but
I think this is working. Any comments appreciated. (YES, I KNOW THAT NATURAL LIGHT IS BEST, BUT SOMETIMES JUST NOT POSSIBLE).

### Ronald Waters — 2019-03-20T05:00:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-box-therapy-experiences.16582/post-265957>

That's an interesting hack. I like the idea of adding some red light, I think the Luminette would be too bright for me, but the question in an N=1 game is if it is working for you. Small steps in a consistent direction will soon have us in a place we have never been before.
